Name: News of the World
Review:
Tom Hanks is easily the best part of this movie. Hanks is very likable in any role he plays, and this movie is no exception. I enjoyed seeing Captain Kidd and Johanna bond. I also thought the cinematography made this movie much more interesting than it would have been otherwise. Many shots of the Texas countryside were breathtaking.

This movie would have been so much better with a better script. This movie is filled with conveniences, cliches, and cheese. This movie played it so safe. This time period in American history is very ugly, but this movie delivers it in a clean, easily digestible way. This movie would have hit me much harder if stuff like the racism and poverty were delivered in a more visceral way. This movie was also super predictable. At every point of the movie I could guess where the story was going to go.

While I like what the movie was going for, I also thought some of the themes were delivered awkwardly. Throughout the film, Captain Kidd reads the news to different Texas towns. While I appreciate the message the movie was trying to make with these scenes, the delivery from the crowds listening to the news was so over the top it was impossible to take seriously.

Despite the mediocre script, this was a competently made movie. The enjoyment I got from Tom Hanks character, the settings, and the visuals made me like this movie more than I disliked it. If you want a fun movie you don’t want to think too much about, this isn’t a terrible choice. Just wait until it’s cheaper than 20 bucks.

Name: Promising Young Woman
Review:
This movie is incredible. The acting here from the two leads is top-notch. Carey Mulligan knocks it out of the park here. Her emotional range is incredible and very believable. Bo Burnham, famous for his unique stand-up material, also does a fantastic job.

I also adored the score of this movie. There are some great scenes that are made even better by these tense, uneasy orchestral passages. This provides a horror-movie aesthetic, a great choice.

The story here is super entertaining. From the very beginning the movie pulls you in. By the time the movie ends you don’t know where your two hours went.

What elevates this movie above a fun thriller are the underlying messages. The film focuses on how society treats women that have been sexually abused. While this movie has received some criticism regarding the presentation of these issues, I thought that it was masterfully done.

The film can be very on-the-nose, but I think that this is a special case of this helping this movie rather than hurting it. For example, seemingly every negative thought about women who have accused a man of sexual assault is stated by a character this movie. While this gives some scenes a PSA vibe, I think that the directness here will force people to self reflect in a way that a more subtle approach could not have done.

This movie takes very little time to slow down and focus on the emotional side of these characters. Some have criticized this, claiming that this movie hijacks a sensitive social issue just for shock value, but I think the lack of emotional introspection about the main characters adds layers to what the movie is trying to say. The audience is made aware of the main character’s mental state through logos rather than pathos. While other movies might shove it in your face that a main character is sad, this movie purposefully avoids doing so in order to make a point. If I know from knowledge of the main character’s past why they feel a certain way, why do I need a scene of them crying to somber piano music in order to care as much? This idea portrayed through the film making is also explored through the actual story, which gives the message twice the impact.

All of that said, this was not a perfect movie. I think while there were some cool shots, more could have been done to make this movie visually interesting. This movie also did not emotionally affect me as much as it could have due to the heavier focus on the thriller aspects, though I do appreciate why that decision was made.

Despite those reservations, I highly recommend this movie. This movie has rubbed some people the wrong way, but I think more conversations about a movie like this, positive or negative, are great to have.

Name: The Trial of the Chicago 7
Review:
This is a consistently entertaining movie. Aaron Sorkin, the writer and director, is famous for making witty and fast-paced dialogue. This movie is no exception. Sorkin balanced seriousness with humor well throughout the script. There were some scenes that made me laugh out loud to myself. There were other scenes that made me very uncomfortable, all thanks to great writing.

I also thought the film had a great energy throughout. I was very rarely bored. There were a few great montages throughout the movie, my favorite being the opening montage of each character leading up to the convention.

Another strong aspect of this film are the performances. Sacha Baron Cohen of “Borat” fame does a particularly fantastic job playing a smart but sometimes obnoxious 60’s political pothead. Everyone did a great job making their characters interesting to watch.

This movie is carried by the chemistry of the actors and the engaging script and story. While these are arguably two of the most important things for a movie to do right, this film really suffered from a lack of creativity regarding more technical aspects.

The cinematography, other than some impressively long takes, is nothing spectacular. I feel that more could have been done to portray the chaos of certain scenes, such as flashbacks to the protests, by showing this through the camera work.

I was also very disappointed by both the soundtrack and the score. While sometimes, like in the opening montage, the music did a great job at bringing some energy, the music added very little other than that. The score here is very generic, and the soundtrack is extremely underutilized. How can a 60’s political drama have a boring soundtrack?

This movie has a strange tone. While the implications of this trial are very serious, as well as the general political situation of the time, this movie seems much more focused on making an entertaining story than a challenging one. There is some very disturbing content here, but little is done through how the movie is presented to make the audience feel as disgusted as they should. In this way, I feel like the subject matter is slightly squandered.

That being said, I liked this movie much more than I disliked it. This is a thrilling ride with a compelling and important story. There is a lot of great humor, made even better by many great performances. What holds this movie back is a lack of creative style. If you want an entertaining courtroom drama with snappy dialogue and electric performances, that’s exactly what you’ll get with “The Trial of the Chicago 7”.

Name: One Night in Miami...
Review: This is a great movie. The best aspect of this movie is the acting. Every single actor knocks it out of the park here. This film does a great job exploring these famous people and why they do what they do. Kingsley Ben-Adir in particular is incredible as Malcolm X. He displays so much emotional range throughout the movie. Ben-Adir has a bright future ahead of him, considering that this is one of his first staring roles. Leslie Odom Jr. of Hamilton fame also completely kills it. A lot of the conflict throughout the film has to do with how differently these two in particular view the movement. There are some incredible scenes of back and fourth between these two men. Both Hodge and Goree are amazing too. The dialogue in this movie is so good. Whereas in “The Trial of the Chicago 7” there were some very “Hollywood” moments, this film is able to be interesting and entertaining without obviously sounding like a movie. When the film really gets going, I could not get enough of these conversations. This film also does a great job at showing how each character interacts when they are alone with another character. For example, when Sam Cooke and Cassius Clay are alone, we hear a very different side to Cooke than when he is in the group. There is also a lot of creativity to both the music and the camerawork. The score by Terence Blanchard is fantastic. His sparse piano pieces really add to this movie’s aesthetic. I also appreciate how the songs of this movie come from the events of the movie, creating an even more immersive experience. The camerawork is creative as well, especially considering a lot of this movie takes place in a hotel room. My biggest problem with this movie is the pacing. While I appreciate this movie giving context to each person’s life before the night in question begins, the beginning is still a little slow. My favorite part of this movie is seeing these characters interact, but it takes a little bit to get to that point. It takes a few minutes to get the ball rolling, but this movie really picks up when we get to the hotel room. This movie is filled with fantastic performances, smart dialogue, and creative decisions. Even with the slow start, this movie left a heavy emotional impact by the time the credits rolled. If you are interested in a dialogue-heavy film with fantastic performances and social commentary, don’t miss out on “One Night in Miami”. https://pchcorral.com/14793/entertainment/one-night-in-miami-is-packed-with-electrifying-performances/ APV

Name: Nomadland
Review:
“Nomadland” was made in an unorthodox way. Rather than having actors play all of the parts, most of the characters here play fictionalized versions of themselves. These nomads Fern meets throughout the movie actually live life the way they do in the movie. Fern goes to an event called the “Rubber Tramp Rendezvous”, which is led by American nomadic influencer Bob Wells. This event is real, and the real Bob Wells plays himself in this story. By making the movie this way, this movie feels as much like a documentary as a fictional story.

This movie structurally is also unique. Rather than having a beginning, middle, and end, this film feels much more like a series of loosely connected vignettes. The film focuses on Fern with many characters going in and out of Fern’s life. This is a clever way of adding more to the realistic, documentary vibe the production of this movie provides.

Frances McDormand as Fern is unbelievable. The strongest aspect of this movie is its realism, and this movie could not have accomplished that without a stunning performance from her. Fern feels just as real as the other real people in the movie. That being said, having real people play themselves did so much for this movie emotionally. When people talk throughout this movie, it hits so much harder knowing that these stories are real. I wonder what the script of this movie looks like, because there is a ton of dialogue here that feels so natural and genuine. I really hope more movies are made like this.

Even with such a realistic presentation, “Nomadland” does an incredible job building tension. Fern throughout the film is constantly searching for work. She has little money, few possessions, and few close friends. From the very beginning, this film makes you very emotionally attached to Fern. This dynamic creates some scenes that are just as suspenseful as any action movie I have ever seen.

The reasons I don’t love this movie are entirely personal. Because this movie doesn’t have a specific beginning, middle, and end, I feel like this movie did not end with as much impact as it could have with more structure. I understand that this movie is going for realism, so it wouldn’t necessarily make sense to have a satisfying ending, but I still think this movie meandered around a little too long by the end.

Personally, I also appreciate it when technical aspects of a movie add to what the movie is trying to say. As a result of this focus on realism, the camera work and music do more to enhance the aesthetic than the themes. This is not an incorrect choice, I just wish this movie had more going on under the surface.

While I think this movie is extremely impressive, this is not my favorite movie I have seen so far. This movie is beautiful, emotional, and brings a feeling I have never felt watching anything else. I completely understand why many people see this as a masterpiece. This movie had a vision and accomplished that vision flawlessly. I highly recommend that everyone checks this one out. It might blow you away more than me.

Name: Minari
Review:
I love this movie. I am so impressed with how this film balanced comfort with conflict. This manages to be both a heartwarming movie about family while being a realistic story about economic hardship, immigration, and faith. This film is bursting at the seams with heart in every aspect possible.

This film made me care so much about this family. The performances here are stellar across the board. The father, played by Steven Yeun, does such a fantastic job portraying a struggling but stoic father. My two favorite characters here are easily the grandmother, played by Youn Yuh-jung, and the boy, played by Alan Kim. The boy meets his grandmother for the first time here, and seeing their relationship develop throughout the film warms my heart just thinking about it. This movie could not have felt as warm and lively without these performances.

What really sets “Minari” apart is its presentation. This movie looks and sounds so gorgeous. The score is immaculate. The songs all have a distinct, warm vibe to them. It sounds like stepping outside to the first day of spring. The music combined with stunning camera work made this movie so interesting to watch during passages of little to no dialogue. There are some shots and music combinations I can still vividly imagine. I really hope this movie gets a nomination for both score and cinematography.

After watching the end of the film I felt conflicted. The story did not go in the direction I was expecting, but the more I think about it the more I appreciate how this film subverts expectations. If I had to make a critique, I feel like the themes of this movie could have more satisfyingly been constructed together. I could be missing something though, so take my reservations with a grain of salt.

There’s a lot to love about this movie. It is funny, heartwarming, shocking, stressful, and everything in-between. I highly recommend this one to everyone.

Name: Sound of Metal
Review:
This movie knocks it out of the park with its story. I think the themes of addiction and loss are presented in a mature and realistic way. There weren’t any melodramatic “movie” moments. There isn’t a sappy piano song when the characters are sad. This movie relied on the acting and the script for emotional impact, and both of these are perfectly executed. I also love how much can be taken away from the movie completely unrelated to deafness. This film is really about coping with change. I love how this movie has an impactful story about a man coping with losing their hearing, but also gives the audience a lot to chew on in a more general sense.

Riz Ahmed absolutely nailed it as Ruben. He has a subtle yet powerful way of portraying his emotion here. There are some scenes where he goes all out, but most of the time his eyes do such a mesmerizing job of communicating how he feels. I also love how likable he is. I want to be friends with this dude, which makes the emotional impact of the movie that much stronger. Riz Ahmed is a name to look out for for the future.

I also love the movie’s portrayal of deafness. The film casts numerous real deaf actors, which obviously adds to the realism. There are numerous scenes here of what it is like to live as a deaf person, and many of them are super interesting to watch. For example, there is a dinner scene from the point of view of Ruben, where many deaf people are communicating as they eat. At first, you can’t hear anything and all you can see are the sign language and mouth movements. Then, the film gives you a point of view without deafness, where you hear the chewing noises and the aggressive banging on the table and other stuff like that. This scene and many others were super interesting to watch. I feel like I learned a lot about the deaf lifestyle from this film.

This film is also technically superb. There are tons of beautiful shots throughout the film of the settings and the characters. I also love the purposefully claustrophobic shots on Ruben during certain scenes. This gives a lot of room for Ahmed to really shine as an actor. The editing and sound design is also really well done. I love how the film portrays Ruben’s hearing loss. The movie often is presented from the perspective of Ruben, which means what he hears we hear. This is masterfully presented throughout the film. I also love how the editing choices from scene to scene make you focus on sound. There are many cuts from quiet places to loud places, or loud places to quiet places, constantly bringing attention to how much information your ears passively take in.

As much as I love this movie, it was not perfect. I think that the movie loses a little momentum around two thirds of the way through. I understand why the filmmakers made this choice, but I wish the pacing was more consistent throughout. That being said, the final scene of this film is incredible.

I was blown away by this movie. Riz Ahmed gives the best performance I’ve seen so far. Even without Riz, the cinematography, story, editing, and everything else make this a worthy contender for Best Picture. Don’t skip “Sound of Metal”.
APV

Name: Ma Rainey's Black Bottom
Review:
Before watching, I had heard a lot of hype about Chadwick Boseman’s performance. Even with high expectations, I was blown away. Levee is flawed, complex character, and I can’t imagine a performance better than Chadwick’s. There are two very different sides to this character, and both of them are executed perfectly. Boseman radiates hurt and pain so convincingly. He also does such a great job being smooth and charismatic. He also completely nails the voice, even when he’s going all out with emotion. There are more than a few monologues here from him that are utterly captivating, especially when he talks about his past. I really hope he wins Best Actor. There is not a moment of imperfection from him.

Viola Davis as Ma Rainey also completely killed it. She portrays someone with a big personality extremely well. I think she also brings a lot of nuance to the character, in particular in one of the final shots of the movie. Everyone else did a great job as well.

I enjoyed the film, but my grievances can be summed up by one question: why is this a movie? I am super happy that August Wilson’s plays, including Fences, are becoming more well-known as a result of making his plays into movies, but my problem is that there is nothing about this movie that a play would not have accomplished. This is a great play, but stuff like camerawork and editing were largely underutilized. This movie has great performances and a thoughtful script, but there is so much lost potential here. After watching this movie, I think I would have rather seen a recording of the actual play with these actors in their roles.

That being said, I am still glad this movie exists because it exposed me to a story I likely wouldn’t have seen otherwise. I just wish there was more here as a film. Regardless, this movie is worth the price of admission for Chadwick’s performance alone. If you want a great story with incredible acting, check this one out.
Netflix

Name: Judas and the Black Messiah
Review:
Both Kaluuya and Stanfield kill it in their roles. After watching footage of Fred Hampton, I have to praise both Kaluuya and the script for the accuracy of Hampton’s mannerisms. Kaluuya does a fantastic job portraying both the political, public persona of Hampton, but he also does a great job in more subtle moments. While I haven’t seen all of his movies, this is a very different performance from his in movies like “Get Out” or “Sicario”. This film cements him in my mind as one of the best actors working right now. Stanfield also does an amazing job portraying a conflicted, manipulated FBI informant. Throughout the film, Stanfield has to both portray power and fear as he attempts to blend into the party. He made me really feel bad for him, despite his actions throughout the film. I am disappointed that both of these actors were nominated for Best Supporting Actor rather than lead. These are the two leads of the film, it is a shame neither got all the recognition they deserve.

I love how the story portrays Fred Hampton and the Black Panther Party. Showing the Black Panther Party from the inside is an excellent way to set the record straight about what the party’s goals were, where their militancy came from, and how reprehensibly they were treated. The way the ideas of the Black Panther Party are shown in conjunction with William O’Neil’s story gives the audience a lot to chew on thematically.

I am also impressed with how faithfully the story of Fred Hampton is told. Both Fred Hampton’s partner and son acted as consultants during production. From my research, the vast majority of this film is exactly how the story happened, which makes this story even more insane and disturbing. The FBI and J. Edgar Hoover were disgusting.

The score to this film is flawless. It reminds me of movies like “The Shining” or “There Will Be Blood”, where often times pure noise is used to enhance the tone. There are also some fantastic orchestral arrangements throughout. During one of my favorite parts of the film, the tension is super high, and all there is to the score is an upright bass. The minimalism here does a fantastic job at creating a sense of dread.

After watching this film, it makes me like “The Trial of the Chicago 7” even less. “Judas and the Black Messiah” has so much more depth to its characters, storytelling, and themes than Trial. It’s disappointing that Sorkin’s film, which is focuses on making witty dialogue and a fun story, will likely do much better this Oscar season than a film as creatively daring as King’s.

This film is almost flawless. Shaka King does an incredible job, especially considering this is only his second feature length film. I think if anything holds this film back, it’s that the style is not consistently executed. There are so many scenes where both the substance and style are perfect. There are others where many aspects are accomplished well, but the same energy and creativity is not quite there. This is extremely nit-picky, but considering how incredible many scenes were, I think Shaka King has a better film in him. I am extremely excited to see King hone his style for the future.

I loved this film. The acting is incredible from both Stanfield and Kaluuya, the story is insane and presented with an engaging style, the film is edited perfectly for building tension, and the music was phenomenal. This is a disturbing watch, but I wouldn’t have it any other way. “Judas and the Black Messiah” is the best “Best Picture” nominee I have seen so far.

Review:
Some conflicting thoughts here. I might just need to rewatch/read about this, but I feel like this realllllllly dragged in the beginning. My favorite parts of this movie were filled with suspense, and that doesn't really happen for a while. If this were going for more of an emotional, serious thing, I think the characters were poorly developed in the large portion of this movie before the story really starts. 

That being said, I was still blown away from a lot of this movie. It definitely got better as it went along. Incredible camera work, loved the score, extremely thrilling (I yelled to myself a lot watching this), just did not think the characters did the filmmaking justice. The second half of this movie CARRIED the first half. I also really love the ending and the decisions they made with the story. Overall I would recommend it, just think it could probably be 20 minutes shorter.

HBO Max

Great time w Makayla tho

HBO Max

Name: The Prestige
Review:

APV

Name: Halloween
Review:
This guy made like five songs and was like yep this is enough

APV

Name: American Psycho
Review:
Way funnier than I was expecting... Christian Bale was obviously unbelievable here, I was consistently entertained, but this didn't blow my mind? Maybe there's more to this than I'm picking up. Still really good though. 

APV

Name: Easy Rider
Review:
WOW, blown away, I love how consistently visually interesting this is. Such a vibe. Also, the editing in this is crazy. Love how the rug is pulled out from under you here before the end. 

HBO Max

Name: Phantom Thread
Review:
This PTA guy ain't half bad!

HBO Max

Name: Shrek
Review:
What a disturbing watch. Disgusting. 

Hulu

Name: The World's End
Review:
Underrated. So energetic and funny (it is Edgar Wright after all). Such a good time, want to rewatch already

DVD

Name: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
Review:
Loved this. So creative and fully takes advantage of all aspects of movie making. Tore me apart, but in a nice way? 

APV

Name: Perfect Blue
Review:
Such a unique, disorienting experience. My rating is subject to change -- I am so lost after a first watch. Mind: bended. If there isn't more to it than just "oh she's dissociating hard" then I don't think it deserves this score. I'd love to see this again to try to figure out what's going on. Regardless, so happy I watched this. 

APV

Name: Blade Runner 2049
Review:
Masterpiece. Visuals and sounds are some of the best of all time. Just LOOK AT THE COLORS. K is literally like my favorite character ever. Probably the greatest sequel of all time. It's unbelievable to me that it rivals the first considering how special that movie is to so many people. This blows that out of the water imo, and I love the original. Jared Leto was put on this earth for this role. He's probably not even acting. 

Blu-Ray

Name: No Country for Old Men
Review:
Such incredible suspense built here. I was consistently entertained and interested in what would happen, but emotionally this didn't do much for me. I'm too young for this lol 

HBO Max

Name: Mikey and Nicky
Review:
Conflicted. I love the sense of humor. Wasn't super engaged the entire time, though. Nicky suuuuuuuuuuuuucks, and so does Mikey. I think this works best as a comedy, I don't really agree that this is as deep as people are saying. This DOES capture a certain kind of friendship in a strangely accurate way, which I also appreciated. 

HBO Max

Name: Unbreakable
Review: There's a lot of skill here in a lot of ways -- so many fantastic shots, a purposeful color pallette, fantastic music, great tension, etc. The biggest strength here, funnily enough, is probably the direction. I think this movie really needed some edits on the script. There's some really stilted dialogue and weird scenes that make little sense. Not to mention the gaping plot holes here. (SPOILER: Really? He NEVER noticed he never got hurt? Never got sick?? What a weirdo). Still, really loved this! I would recommend it

Name: The Favourite
Review:
I didn't like this quite as much as, uh, the deer one, but I still really enjoyed this! It's impressive how complicated all of the characters motives are. Brilliant acting all around emphasizes their complexity. Also, this is beautifully shot. Stunning. Made me want the ability to watch it in 4K. Also, so hilarious. I love his absurdist deadpan thing he has going on. Unfortunately, this didn't move me much emotionally. I also didnt pick up on many interesting themes other than that regarding the characters motivations. Personally, I'm the least into movies like that compared to more plot or theme driven stuff sooooo maybe this isn't the Yorgos for me! 

APV

Name: When Harry Met Sally...
Review:
So entertaining, funny, heartfelt, and sweet. Harry and Sally feel like actual people. This isn't the most complicated or mind blowing thing you'll ever see, but who cares? This slaps

HBO Max

Name: Edward Scissorhands
Review: I can't get behind Tim Burton, idk why. This feels like a worse The Elephant Man. Also, I feel like this really leaned on the score to create the atmosphere, but I just wasn't a fan. I really felt bad for the guy, but I don't normally like movies where the whole thing is look at how nice this guy is NOW IM GOING TO TORTURE YOU. I like how it parodied suburban life too with the look of the neighborhood, but I don't think the social commentary was anything other than gossip is bad, don't judge a book by it's cover, etc. Also, Eddy boy, you put yourself in so many avoidable situations. SPOILER: Why were you all over the son? Why didn't you explain yourself? Silly silly man APV

Name: Le Samouraï
Review:
I'm not going to pretend that I was super entertained watching this, but MAN the second half of the film especially gave me a lot to love. This Adam Neely lookin dude is unbelievable here. His performance is so subtle, but purposeful. I also love the use of color here. One of the greatest endings I've ever seen. Not sure I'll ever want to watch this again, but I'm glad I saw this. Prepare for a slow one

HBO Max

Name: Pi
Review:
An exercise in style over substance, but the style is so up my alley. The energy here is so crazy and weird. This made no freaking sense, but it allowed for DarryAir to really let loose from a directing perspective. So many unsettling and creative shots. If you want an entertaining, incoherent mess, look no further than this. 

APV

Name: Black Swan
Review: Is this movie amazing? Absolutely. Natalie Portman absolutely killed it, she really deserved that Oscar. The use of mirrors here is also super creative and thoughtful. There were so many scenes where the positions of the characters in reflections were so impressive that it distracted me from the dialogue. This is also paced perfectly. That being said, the similarities between this movie and "Perfect Blue" are really, really annoying. I know that these movies are slightly different, with one focusing more on the treatment of women in media and the other one focusing more on unhealthy obsession (though both films explore both of these topics), but the genius of "Black Swan" would have been so much more impressive without "Perfect Blue". I think this film is better in certain ways (this is more consistently entertaining and disturbing), but this still feels like a watered down "Perfect Blue". Still though, fantastic watch, Aronofsky is one of my favorite directors.

Name: RoboCop
Review: I'm blown away by this. This is smart, hilarious, disgusting, balls-to-the-wall sci-fi action. When I think of an action movie from the 80 s from now on, I'll judge it based on how close it gets me to Robocop. EXTREMELY underrated. Beats Terminator, Mad Max, and Aliens any day of the week. APV

Name: Saving Private Ryan
Review:
What a feat on an effects level. The shooty shooty bang bang scenes were harrowing, disgusting, and so intense. There's a lot to this other than the shooty bang pew pew too, but at certain points I feel that the message was oversimplified regarding what it means to do the pew pew on each other. I understand the appeal, but a few more thematic things held this back a teasny weansy. 

APV

Name: Rocky
Review:
Man, this almost made me cry. Rocky is one of the most likeable characters I've ever seen, this is pretty masterful character development. This is almost perfect from a character level (man, if only there wasn't that rapey scene). I do remember when i first watched this the montage being more impressive. Now, it's meh for me tbh. The music was great, but from a filmmaking perspective I feel that this wasn't that impressive. Still, total classic for the Stallone performance and the story, can't recommend it enough. 

HBO Max

Name: Mean Girls
Review:
I think this really shined in the middle. The beginning and end felt run of the mill, but especially Lohan's descent in to mean girl-dom was hilarious. Really funny scenes, but wasn't super consistent. 

APV

Name: Rushmore
Review: The story is all over the place, but by making it so, Wes Anderson is able to use the story as a vehicle for developing his style. This feels like Aronofsky's Pi in the same way. Not my favorite, but still so much to love. The O.R. line is pure gold. APV

Name: Exit Through the Gift Shop
Review:
This film ingeniously illustrates the perversion of street art by mainstream attention and big money through telling the story of its demise through the eyes of someone that didn't understand the movement from the very beginning. This is an enthralling documentary just from the story alone, but this also gives the audience a lot to chew on if they desire. 

APV

Name: Prisoners
Review:
My god. This is the first time in a long while I feel completely drained from a film. Emotionally, physically, mentally, everything. These characters are so beautifully developed. The story is completely bonkers and unpredictable. The religious backdrop and imagery are flawlessly executed. The mood is perfectly dreary. D.V. (sorry dude I'll just never comprehend your name) is a master at both atmosphere and evoking intense emotion from simple things. Completely gripped from beginning to end. I wish more movies were like this. AND DON'T EVEN GET ME STARTED ON THE ACTING MAN WOW. Oscars for everyone please thank you. 

Hulu

Name: Seven Samurai
Review: Incredible. The camera work and production aspects of this movie are so unbelievably executed. The battle scenes man idk how they did it. There are also so many characters and concepts touched on that intrigued me a lot. That being said, this is far from my thing. Not a big fan of movies where the action set pieces are the meat and potatoes of the movie. I think that causes for the film to inherently age poorly. While I'm sure in '54 I would have been astonished, I left this wishing the story went in a different direction. Glad I watched, but not my favorite.

Name: Zodiac
Review:
Gripping from beginning to end. The runtime felt completely justified here considering the length of time this movie encompasses. This is an entertaining watch with great characters, in particular Gyllenhaal's, but this didn't bring enough to the table thematically to call this a fantastic film. That being said, if you're into mystery thrillers, this is a must watch. 

Paramount+

Name: Thief
Review:
What a great film. The aesthetic is oozing in every scene. This feels like Drive except, like, the real thing. This is easily one of the greatest scores of any movie I've ever heard. The camerawork and colors as well are top notch. I love what this was going for, and I think in context to what it was going for it was a perfect movie. The ending was also sooooooooooo well done. Personally, I think this could have hit me much harder emotionally had they gone a slightly different direction with the tone, but I'm not super mad about it. Regardless, this is a masterclass in style. 

HBO Max

Name: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
Review:
Part of me sees this as an impressive avant garde depiction of a drug bender with fantastic performances and a creative, unique tone. The other part of me sees this as Gilliam telling the leads to jump around and make funny noises while he puts as much goofy stuff in front of the camera as physically possible. All I know for sure is that this is a wild ride

🏴‍☠️

Name: The Terminator
Review:
There are some great ideas in here, but man is this overrated. I think that The Terminator (the dudeborg) is menacing, and the ending is pretty great, but man is Reese dull. This is my second watch and I literally didn't recognize him when he came on the screen. I think this movie needed to give me more reason to care about these characters to facilitate the tension this goes for. There are so many better movies from this era. I hope T2 is better. 

Hulu

Name: She's All That
Review:
Forgettable for the most part. There were some cool transitions and surprisingly long shots, especially in the beginning, but there was sooooooo much filler. Loses half a star for the pube eating scene. Wtf was that. Also, what teenage girl would leave Freddie for Shaggy. Also, why can't there be a movie where the girl is beautiful and has glasses like whyyyy. Not the worst thing ever though I wasn't ever bored. 

APV

Name: Climax
Review: I'm being really generous with this rating. Technically this is a masterpiece. The long shots, intricate choreography, eerie lighting, and on-point editing all contributed to a crazy experience. That being said, this did not connect to me on a personal level whatsoever. This film really exemplifies my problems I had with his longer, superior film Enter the Void: it feels like there's no point. Maybe that IS the point, but c'mon, you can't just slap some big brain quotes about how crazy it is we're born and we die to make it meaningful. I think Gasper Noe is one of the most interesting and mind bending directors working today, but I'd love to see him do something with more complex emotion. Day after edit: NVM this deserves this rating this was a crazy experience I can't stop thinking about it APV

Name: The Piano Teacher
Review:
This sure is a Hanake film. Isabelle Hubert is so unbelievably good here. Hanake loves his long shots, and she really makes the most of them. This is also able to be both unexciting and extremely gripping. I think the themes are artfully and thoughtfully explored. This might actually be my least favorite of the three I've seen, but man this guy knows what he's doing when it comes to creating a cohesive thematic experience. Incredible ending. 

APV

Name: Upstream Color
Review: Wow. I absolutely LOVE this. This, similarly to his previous film Primer, will undoubtedly make you very confused, even though the "solution" here is much simpler than for Primer. That being said, this is a vastly different experience for me in ways I did not anticipate. This film does such an incredible job at creating emotional weight even without me having any understanding of the plot. I'm normally not into movies that leave such a wide gap for interpretation, but the beauty of the score and the shots and the (very confusing) story all contributed to such a limitless feeling I haven't felt since watching Enter the Void. This felt more like an experience than a movie, I feel changed. I really want to give this 5 stars, but I want to make sure it holds up on another watch (I think it will). P.S., trigger warning, someone used a metal fork to cook eggs in a Teflon pan. What kind of savagery is that. Arrow Blu-ray

Name: Before Midnight
Review:
This feels like the most complex of the Before Trilogy for me. I really love the direction this one went, it turned the whole urgent love thing on it's head in a way. I could listen to Jesse and Céline talk forever. I think these characters choices and personalities are so well developed. I also love how the film slowly develops what happened in the movie through dialogue in a really subtle way. This isn't as impressive technically as the other two (there are cool things though, like the camera movement when characters talked midconvo) but I think the emotional weight is stronger than the second and almost up to the first. Loved it! Strong 8/10

APV

Name: F for Fake
Review:
This is everything I was hoping for: engaging, surprising, and full of technical flair. This was a little confusing in the beginning, but even then the editing and interwoven acting and sets all kept the movie interesting almost the whole runtime. Orson Welles seems like the coolest dude ever, I wish I could have met him. I love how the filmmaking here reflects the content of the story, and how Welles even becomes self reflective. I know understand why neckbeards wear fedoras: they think they look like Orson Welles in this movie. I would love to watch this one again. 

HBO Max

Name: La La Land
Review:
Not a musical (like, movie musical) person, never been one. This completely KNOCKED my SOCKS off. Such rich and complex characters, a killer performance from Emma Stone and a great one from Ryan Gosling, BEAUTIFUL colors, breathtakingly long takes, and a heart-wrenching story. This is such a top tier ending, easily in my top 5 ever. Such a vibrant, lively film. This director has shot way up near the top of my list. I need to see this again!!

APV

Name: The 400 Blows
Review:
Even watching this with awful allergies was great! The score, acting, and beautiful cinematography all culminated in such a personal slice of life experience. I really feel like I know this boy. Not the best movie of all time or anything but I get the hype. 

HBO Max

Name: Badlands
Review:
This is the most tonally strange film I think I've ever seen. This feels like a proto-Wes Anderson movie in it's dreamy sheen, serene music, and emotional detachment, but all of that style over a story about a deranged killer. This is a beautifully shot film with a great score, but what really makes this film so interesting to me is the weird tone. It really portrays the detachment of the main characters from their situation well, even though their breed of detachment is very different.  There's some questionable acting and decision making from the characters here, but this absolutely deserves classic status. 

HBO Max

Name: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
Review:
Teetering on a 4. This was SO MUCH BETTER than T1. The cinnamon topography was unbelievably good for so much of the movie. This also did a great job at creatively expanding the Terminator universe. The pacing, while not perfect, was the biggest improvement from the first film. Also, Sarah Connor is much more interesting here. If only that kid didn't have such terrible lines, and if some really silly things didn't happen, this would be much closer to a masterpiece. 

Blu-ray

Name: House
Review:
I wish movies like this were made today. I feel like every idea anyone on set had must have been instantly greenlit. This does not hold back, in particular with the deaths and the general style. I had a blast watching this, easily one of my favorite "horror" movies ever. I love how this both works as a good ol' time and as a creative, boundary-pushing experience. I thought I might be laughing AT this, but I was genuinely laughing WITH this throughout. Brilliant. 

HBO Max

Name: Moonrise Kingdom
Review:
THIS IS MY FAVORITE WES ANDERSON EASYYYYYY. This is perfectly paced, scored, and shot. This story and setting is absolutely PERFECT for Wes and he really knocked it out of the park. I laughed out loud multiple times, which I can't say for all his other films. I think that emotionally and character developmentally there could be slight improvements, this is so close to being a masterpiece. I LOVE this movie. 

Criterion Blu-ray

Name: What We Do in the Shadows
Review:
Taika Waititi is the best. I really liked this! It was consistently funny and they did a lot with the concept. This does drag a bit at some points, but overall very happy with it. Stu CARRIED. 

Plex

Name: Boogie Nights
Review:
REALLY loved this. The characters are so interesting to watch. The settings, both the 70s and the 80s, are nicely developed. The real stars of the movie though are the camera movement and the unbelievable tension building. In the beginning, it was more about wanting Dirk to succeed, and in the second half it was...different tension. That being said, this is my least favorite PTA by far. The pacing wasn't quite right. The transition from the first half to the second half felt kind of rocky (though the very end of the 70s was craaaaaazy). I also felt way less emotionally affected due to story decisions. If I were smarter I might understand more why those decisions were made, but I'm not, sooooo 4/5

Netflix

Name: The Green Knight
Review:
This is a great movie to see in the theater! I haven't been since, you know, so certain elements of this movie like the haunting score, the astounding, almost BR2049 visuals, and the chaotically colored camerawork really popped. That being said, this was a much bigger emotional/visual experience for me than a plot one. Partially because I couldn't understand what the hell they were saying half the time, but partially due to how dreamlike these events are. I am super excited to read people's interpretations, but still at certain points this felt more like a throw everything at the wall and see what sticks thing. Also, the intensity of the score and visuals was hilariously misused in the beginning of the film. It made more sense later on, but when this guy is just running around town, the almost horror sounding score was so out of place. This is a fragile 8. 

MARCUS THEATER WOOOOO

Name: A Single Man
Review: I love so much about A Single Man. Firth delivers such a nuanced and heartbreaking performance. The scene on the phone was stunning -- the little twitches on his face carried so much weight. I also adored the score and use of color. Unfortunately, I wasn't a massive fan of the look of the movie. The cinematography felt purposeless and cramped for no reason. I also feel like this would have benefitted from less jumpy editing. Longer shots in many scenes could have emphasized the dullness and emptiness George felt. Still, the emotional heart of the movie is gold. Netflix

Name: Platoon
Review:
So many beautiful and harrowing shots throughout. Willem Dafoe completely stole the show for me. I also really appreciate how (to a certain extent) this film humanizes the enemy, rather than a "good guy pew pew bad guy" thing. What took me out of the movie were both the set designs and the dialogue. Almost every scene felt so cramped, it gave me a sitcom vibe. All these characters also talk so obnoxiously also, too cheesy for the subject matter. Still, this is a pretty thoughtful war movie with a couple great performances. 

APV

Name: Beverly Hills Cop
Review:
Didn't do much for me in the first half, but when the dynamics with Judge's character and that other dude who's name I won't look up began it got much funnier for me. Pretty cheesy, very 80s, but a good time nonetheless. Also appreciate the lack of a romantic interest! Eddie Murphy is so likeable. 

HBO Max

Name: Now You See Me
Review:
Really this movie is just famous actors play themselves while crazy magic shit happens that made no sense and it was crazy and hilarious and exhilarating and so so so silly. There were some cool things about it actually, like the switching between the commercial airline and the private jet editing, and the scene where (spoilers for the rest of the sentence) the chase scene happens where they escape the building was genuinely fun. Great fun plot ideas, zero character development and a twist that makes ZERO sense, yeah it was fun. 

APV

Name: Split
Review:
M Night, from what I've seen, is his own worst enemy. He has so many brilliant ideas that get completely squandered by really dumb decisions that could be easily fixed. The dialogue here is super stilted. Joy's character is horribly mishandled. These characters make baffling decisions. And yet, even with all those problems, I loved this!!! The opening credits really set the table for a creepy tone that perferated the whole film. James McAvoy delivers the definition of a dynamic performance. If I had to bet money on a future Oscar winner, he'd be near the top (if he starts getting some actually good roles). A montage near the end of the movie really had me immersed in the tension. This is such a cool concept, and while they do a lot of cool things with it, I still feel like they could have done just a touch more. Great watch! M. Night, you need someone to quality check your stuff, because you clearly can't tell your genius from your junk. 

APV

Name: Mulholland Drive
Review: This was a very frustrating watch for me. I really love Lynch's style. There are no other movies that make me as unsettled walking around my dark house as his do. I was really looking forward to this one because of how much I loved his abstract storytelling for Lost Highway. Unfortunately, as I was watching it, I can't say I had a fun time. It felt like a constant cycle of 15 minutes of anticipation for an ounce of confusion-relief. You learn what's going on very, very slowly. That being said, I understand it's status as a masterpiece. The tone and mood here are so uniquely unsettling. The lighting and the colors and the roaming camera movement all felt very purposeful at developing that mood. Also, after the movie when I could read theories from smarter people than me, I was blown away at what this film is actually doing. I really would love to watch this again, but as of right now, I can't say this is one of my favorites (though I really loved it!!) APV

Name: The Suicide Squad
Review:
INCREDIBLE in many ways, kind of stupid in many ways too. Visually this is a total feast -- I love how little this film holds back with the grusome kills and such, it is much more in line with the concept. I also loved the music and the color palate. The cinematography was really cool as well. I really liked a scene where a fight scene is shown through the reflection of a helmet. There are other choices similarly creative throughout the movie. Where this one really falters for me is the characters and the humor. This wasn't that funny, and half the characters were straight up annoying. Margot Robbie is a fantastic actress, but she sucks here. Idris Elba was great. The dialogue was not very good, often kind of cringy. Some funny parts, but this works much better as an absurd action movie with visual comedy elements than a straight up comedy. Needed less talking. Also so many plot things were so stupid in ways that didn't serve to purposefully make the movie absurd. Mixed feelings on this one and soooooooo overrated. 

HBO Max

Name: The Green Mile
Review:
Sorry y'all, this was the biggest cheesefest of all time. I think the story in concept is super interesting, but man does it get squandered by the filmmaking. Almost all the characters were played so sillily: the agency-less and characterless right-hand man, the cracked out of his mind Sam Rockwell, the hooping and hollering janitor, the adult version of the "mine" kid from lazytown, and the wife who only exists to have sex with the main character or provide comfort to him. The dialogue could have been from a Hallmark movie half the time. I think Hanks and Duncan do a great job, as well as the guy that speaks french occasionally, but that was it. The score is so tonally inappropriate and bland. The cinematography shines sometimes, like during what could be considered the climax of the movie, but did not provide anything groundbreaking either. Everything above average about this movie regards the story and its thematic implications, but the camera, acting, score, or anything similar took a backseat when it could have driven this film to a much better place. This had so much potential to be something amazing. Could you imagine this under a different director? I would still recommend this one over all, but if you're not into some cheese or 90s movie cliches, just skip it. 

HBO Max

Name: Scott Pilgrim vs. the World
Review:
EDGAR WRIGHT DOESN'T MISS. After a rewatch, this is up there with Hot Fuzz (which is interesting, because these are the only ones I've seen twice). The presentation of this movie is flawless. So many smooth cuts between locations, a perfectly executed comic book feel (how many hipster points do I get if I say this is my favorite comic book movie?), and hilarious visual comedy. This doesn't get a 5 from me because not all the jokes landed like they could have, and Ramona is just not a great character. She feels too much like an objective. Still, this is a fantastically paced ride, highly recommend this if you don't want something too serious. 

Watched at the Shack!

Name: F9
Review: Half watched half of this. Cars went boom

Name: Casino
Review:
Goodfellas, but in Vegas!!! This films presentation is so frantic and in your face (just like a casino!) I thought all of the main characters were interesting, and all of the leads totally killed it. This, unlike Goodfellas, is also super fast paced. So much happened even for a 3 hour movie. Unfortunately I just don't think this kind of Scorsese is completely my thing (I really don't get much enjoyment from getting told all of the details of the crime and the names and what obscure side character mad at who and whatever). Still, I had a total blast! Did not feel like 3 hours. The scene in which a head is in an uncomfortable location was wild. 

Blu-ray

Name: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
Review:
The first half of this movie was so kickass. The bus and building (you'll know) scenes were UNBELIEVABLE. Fantastic action. Also loved both the lead (Simu Liu) and his father (Tony Leung Chiu-Wai) here. I just didn't love where the story went and the action when it came to more mythical stuff on screen. Not a big marvel movie person, but this one was a great origin film. Pretty bland other than some beautiful shots every once in a while. 

Marcus Theaters Chesterfield

Name: Eraserhead
Review: I really liked this, but this is probably my least favorite Lynch so far. I could totally see this growing on me after repeated viewings. Lynch does such a fantastic job creating the most disgusting, unappealing, lifeless atmosphere. The set design is noticeably good. I also appreciate what this movie is doing in trying to portray the uncertainty and irreversibility of fatherhood. This film really loses me at some parts, though. This is Lynch at his least thorough (or what a more positive review might call "most abstract"). I can't say watching a Lynch film is ever an "enjoyable" experience, but understanding theories about what the film is about makes the ride worth-while. I think (despite what Lynch might tell you) that he really does have an idea in his head about what his movies are about, and certain details all come together to make that idea in that Lynchian style (just read theories to Lost Highway and Mulholland Drive). Eraserhead does not seem to have that same intention, which leaves me more with the experience I get watching it, which is really just the spooky vibe. Fantastic spooky vibe, but not much else for me.

Name: Parasite
Review:
Third time watching it, and once again I like it even MORE. This film is arguably flawless. I was blown away this time by how smooth the pacing was, especially considering how many different genres this movie encompasses. I know exactly what happens in this movie and I still couldn't keep myself from screaming at my computer at the suspenseful moments. The first 40 minutes in particular are some of the best filmmaking I think I've ever seen, all culminating in that amazing montage. All the shots are so breathtaking and intentional. I absolutely adore how the social commentary is handled. This is not a black or white movie, but it also doesn't attempt to "both sides" it, if you know what I mean? There's so much sympathy and love for the characters. There are so many moments that just filled me with pure joy watching it. 

SPOILERS AHEAD: 







The way the score is used as the Kim family's rage is building up during the kid's birthday party is so perfect. There is clearly a lot of attention to detail here, it was cool to notice that the Kim family later in the movie ate at the pizza place they were working for at the beginning. I also noticed they put a scar on the son's face for his brain surgery while he's on the subway deciphering the message from his father. I remember in previous watches not LOVING the ending, but man I do now. I think the motif about planning culminated in both a comforting and a disturbing way with the son laying out his honestly unrealistic plan of buying the house his father is hiding in. It both made me feel happy that he felt like he COULD plan but sad knowing that it probably wasn't going to happen. So glad I watched it a third time. I need the Criterion.

Hulu

Name: Polytechnique
Review: Yeah, really mixed feelings on this one. My biggest problem with this film by far is the purpose. I do not understand wanting to make a movie based off of a real event with the only discernible purpose being to show how traumatic it was. This feels completely insensitive at some points. I am not of the belief that you can't make movies about certain things, but MAN this felt exploitative to me. This film is pretty barebones, so the fact that the majority of the focus were scenes of this dude being pissed off and killing people felt artistically useless. This is the kind of film Funny Games is commenting on. That being said, Denis is such a talented director. The sense of foreboding was well done, and there were a few shots that reminded me of BR2049 in how creative and purposeful they felt. This film is well made, but I can't say I recommend it. If you're really starving for Daddy Denis content I guess check it out but uh yeah. Just a bunch of people getting shot for 77 minutes. ((AND NOT BY A CAMERA AMMIRITE))

Name: Incendies
Review: Yeah idk what to even say after that ending. Whoooooowie. Wow. Wow! This is FANTASTIC. This took me a long time to get on board. I was interested and engaged throughout in a similar way to how I was with Prisoners, but I didn't really understand the point until the end. That said, sticks the landing so brilliantly that it retroactively completely changes my attitude about the beginning. There's so much to love here, but what there is to love is different from many of his other films. This story is so engaging and consistently terrifying. There are some truly harrowing scenes, but with what this film is about it feels totally justified. The acting was great, the film looked beautiful, but the consistently engaging story and smart structure of the film really sold this for me. Absolutely top 3 Villeneuve. LOVED IT

Name: Elephant
Review:
If you want to go in COMPLETELY BLIND don't read this, but coming from someone that cares about spoilers, this isn't going to change your viewing experience. 

Alright, so I've heard a lot of people when talking about this movie say things I completely disagree with. One of the things is that the movie doesn't build enough of the characters, or that we aren't made to care about the characters. This is CRAZY to me -- this film does such a wonderful job at developing a large amount of characters in a short amount of time. Are they complex? No, but why do they need to be? These are ordinary people, and for the purposes of this movie, all we need is that these people are real. This is a rare case where quantity actually is better than quality. I have also heard two different opinions: either that the school shooting wasn't disturbing enough, or that this film is exploitative and disgusting. I disagree with both of these takes, the first one because wtf how is this NOT disturbing to you, and the second one because this is a pretty narrow minded way of looking at this film. I got no joy watching the shooting. I felt like the shooting itself was tastefully done, at least as much as it could have been. 

Here's where my problems with this film began: the scenes with the shooters in their normal lives. I ADORE how the scenes of the other students are handled, but man did this movie fall apart for me once it tried showing the shooters. This film throws in all sorts of stuff that people have claimed to cause school shooters (violent videogames, bullying, sexual deviency, etc.), but man does it look pretentious and tasteless to attempt to concoct an "accurate" portrayal of what a school shooters life must be like to cause one to act the way they do. I don't care. There is no value in attempting to show me these people. Is it disturbing? Sure, but there's no greater reason for it. This film would have been so, so much stronger if it was only the highschoolers lives and nothing else. I think the structure of the film, where it's a small amount of time from multiple perspectives, is so so perfect because it emphasizes the amount of life lived in such a massive place as a school. I also think the performances across the board felt so real. I also love the transitions with the clouds, emphasizing the idea of this just being another day. The earth does not stop spinning for such a harrowing tragedy. There are also maybe 4 or 5 really long shots that were super logistically impressive. This could be edited into a near masterpiece, but some key decisions regarding the treatment of the shooters soiled it for me hard. 

APV

Name: Titane
Review:
Not for the faint of heart, for sure. I love (?) how much this movie puts you on edge, it feels like anything can happen at any moment. I also thought the performances throughout were pretty stunning. The body horror stuff was well done. Personally idk if I would want to put myself through this again. Still thinking about what to think about this one. 

Plaza Frontnac

Name: Casino Royale
Review:
Entertaining and thrilling throughout. Mads and Daniel Craig really kept me interested above all else. The story was pretty cool, love the poker vibe thing going on. Pretty stupid sometimes too, especially with the romance. I'm also surprised at how consistently I was entertained at a 2 and a half hour run time. Fantastic action and great acting both make this absolutely worth a watch. 

🏴‍☠️

Name: Hereditary
Review:
LOL what was this. Just want to say I think this is the first horror movie I have watched on my own so go me go me thank you thank you. I only paused it because I was scared once! 

I've heard a lot about the performances in the film, but honestly other than Toni Collette I feel like this was not an amazing cast. The father was alright but his accent (more specifically, whether or not he had one) was very distracting. The son was really questionable in some scenes. He did numb and terrified well but that was about it. The girl was alright. Collette totally killed this though, for the most part (it sometimes felt like she started talking so quickly it was unnatural but idk if I'm just making that up). 

What makes this movie stand out to me is the presentation. Oh my lord this thing looks and sounds and feels so foreboding. There are so many interesting shots and these are some 5/5 transitions between scenes too. I love how some of the shots make the house look really small, making the house look like one of her pieces. The music is also absolutely killer through the whole thing. There's also a specific creepy vibe this movie touches into that I've never felt watching anything else.

The story is also super interesting. There is a scene where someone needs to, uh, be accountable (iykyk) and that scene was presented in such a creative way. This film gave the audience just enough to keep watching throughout, I never felt bored. There's a wild mystery here set up throughout that I love.

Is this a modern horror masterpiece? No, especially because of the performances, but I think this is totally worth a rewatch in the future. Incredible directorial debut. Ari Aster is super exciting

APV

Name: Election
Review:
I love how this film both gives the vibe that nothing in it matters or is important at all while also being a hilarious and pointed satire. I laughed out loud so much throughout. I also "love" (hate) all the characters except Paul he's literally my favorite person ever. Definitely want to rewatch. So good

Hulu

Name: Transit
Review:
This movie's pretty cool!! I'll be honest, just bc brain not work right, I was confused for like 2/3 of the movie about basic plot stuff and I'm sure that severely harmed my enjoyment of the film. I THINK I GOT EVERYTHING NOW THOUGH, andddd wow I love the way this story is presented. There are subtle decisions made about how this story is being told to you such that the stuff like the narration say a lot in how they are used more than what they say. this wasn't a consistently engaging film for me (maybe bc I was so confused) but I can respect the objective tone this film takes on. Sad shit happens and it isn't shown in a way that feels manipulative. This film totally works thematically as both a literal retelling of escaping Nazi Germany as well as in more symbolic ways, brushing on themes about what it means to be the identity of someone (I think this film has a lot of overlap with "Close Up", interestingly). This deserves a rewatch, overall I'd recommend it, just make sure you understand the first 15 minutes lol. 

Showing at Strickland Hall for German Studies week or something idk I came for the movie

Name: Blackfish
Review:
Thought I'd watch a horror movie to be seasonal. Yeah this was terrible. I think this is an example of a super interesting topic with interesting, likeable people interviewed carrying the documentary. This was so hard to watch. I had to back away from my computer a few times. This doesn't communicate anything mind-blowing, like, I'm not surprised at all at the mistreatment of these animals and how SeaWorld wants to kill their trainers for profits and blame them for their deaths and stuff, but that does not make it insane to watch. The editing of the ending pumped it from a 7 to an 8, I genuinely almost cried, which isn't what I was expecting. Free Tilly? (This is kinda old now so I'm going to look up what happened after this)

Hulu

Name: Rosemary's Baby
Review:
Yeah this was not what I expected. I love this! Thinking about it more and more the themes of motherhood and gender roles are so expertly crafted. This is a "horror" movies in obvious ways when you watch it, but it's really about the horror of having no agency, especially as a woman in the 60s. I cannot believe that someone as disgusting as this director would poop out such a progressive, forward-thinking statement. I love the atmosphere of this film, it was so off-putting and creepy. Cassavetes NAILED his performance. Mia Farrow was amazing too, but I feel like she wasn't given as much to work with as his character. So many eerie shots (the phone booth, the dream sequences, etc.) My critiques are pretty personal (I think gaslighting is so boring and frustrating and I hate watching it, the dialogue was a little hokey) so I think I still like Chinatown just a little more, but this is fantastic. I would love to rewatch this one. 

APV

Name: Over the Garden Wall
Review:
This isn't a perfect show, but it's so good in all the right ways. I had such a cozy experience watching this. The scenery, the spookiness, and the score just YES. Laugh out loud funny, so creative, and Greg is just the best. Made me feel like a kid excited for Halloween. I want this on the background 24/7 whenever fall comes from now on. I'm normally not an adventure movie person, but this REALLY delivered on the adventure. I loved seeing where they went and who they met and what they solved and stuff it was so nice. HAPPYYYY

HBO Max

Name: Dune
Review:
Least favorite Daddy Denis since Polytechnique. Visually this is stunning, but not quite as cool as BR2049. If this film did with all it's characters what it did with Oscar Isaac's, I would have liked this a lot more -- these people are booooorrrrring. I didn't believe for a second they were anyone but the actors playing them. This movie also suffered from being the first 10th of a story. The movie ended and I was like wow okay I feel nothing. The political intrigue and characterization that made the book great was not poorly executed, but rather not even attempted. This felt like a beautiful, slow Star Wars movie with amazing music. That's not really what I wanted out of this. That said, my opinion of this film could change if all the movies came out. This just didn't give me enough as a stand alone film to LOVE (even though it did give a lot to like)

Marcus Chesterfield Galaxy

Name: Baby Driver
Review:
Is this one of Edgar Wright's better films? Maybe. It's so impressive, the way the action comes together with the music is so satisfying. Amazing colors and colorful characters and brilliant criminal world building. That said, the emotional side of this movie falls so hard for me. Baby isn't a compelling character for me, and the love interest is even worse in that way. It's like an elevated Fast and Furious movie -- really fun popcorn flick that's expertly crafted, but not much under that. Dunno, I'd rather watch something with a little more heart. Least favorite Wright. 

Blu-Ray

Name: Last Night in Soho
Review:
Watching Edgar Wright's style combined with a horror movie is exactly what it sounds like. The tone was really weird. Like, when scary things happen in this movie it reminds me of his other movies -- it's like he's not taking it completely seriously. This film felt too lighthearted for its own good. Still, I enjoyed myself the majority of the time. I love so many visual sequences (there's this amazing montage of the main character running through a hallway with a bunch of rooms and we see what's in the rooms and it's so dope), but this doesn't have the same meticulousness as his other films. This is easily his sloppiest film, yet this still comes across as more likeable than "Baby Driver" to me. Genuinely brilliant leading performances from ATJ and Thomasin McKenzie, incredible color palate and set designs, and I love thematically the films content (and sort of surprised at it's take). I don't know, this is absolutely a worse movie than baby driver but I liked watching it more. Not sure horror is his strongsuit though.

Marcus Chesterfield Galaxy

Name: Magnolia
Review:
Easily PTA's most ambitious film. He really throws a lot at you here, so many themes, characters, and stylistic choices. I can imagine that this gave him a lot of experience for his future work because there are things in here he focuses more on later. I think the first half of the film is so exhaustingly thrilling, even Safdie-esque, which does not surprise me because of his ability to use music and emotion to overwhelm you in other films. I liked the second half as well, I just don't know that it was as engaging as the first half. I appreciate the direction it went but it still can't help but feel cheap to me? Anyway, I would still love to see this again, the long shots, the exhausting cuts between so many stories, the brilliant music (except for that one singing scene sorry but wtf was that PTA are you kidding me), and the memorable characters. Might be my least favorite PTA so far, but if this is his worst ohhhh my gosh this guy is good. 

Netflix

Name: The Host
Review:
This is a rare film where the shot composition was obnoxiously good -- the framing of so many situations added to the tension and made the film just look cool. Also love the creativity of the story, there was no predicting what would happen. Love the themes touched on of government intervention and social responsibility. The humor here felt very weird tho. Definitely much less smooth integration of genres compared to "Parasite". Still, the characters were fun, the story was fun, and fun fun fun!

APV

Name: Hard Eight
Review:
I love watching directorial debuts that aren't mind-blowing but have the glimpses of what makes their future work so great. The ideas behind the two male leads are pretty interesting but the script falls pretty flat and developing these people gracefully. There were some smooth tracking camera sequences where I was like yup that's PTA. This gave me a little bit of Thief vibes which I wasn't expecting. I had a good time, I loved how meditative the film felt, but I'm not sure I'll ever come back to this. 

APV

Name: The French Dispatch
Review:
This is Wes's strongest visually and weakest emotionally. I can't stress enough how much I love the look of this movie. The look is constantly changing, whether it's the colors or the aspect ratio or the shot composition, yet it never feels distracting. It's so smooth. Personally I didn't love the intro story or the painter story, but I loved the student rebellion story and LOVED the chef story, so it really grew on me as it went. This feels like Wes is making a movie surrounding visuals rather than the other way around, which is fine because I love his style and he totally killed it. I hope he doesn't continue too hard down this direction and returns to the emotional potency of Tenenbaums or Moonrise, but still I am a big fan of this!!

Ragtag Cinema opening night WOOO

Name: Inherent Vice
Review:
Wow, I didn't think PTA had it in him to make such an annoying, obtuse film. Now, I love the cinematography and the colors of course cause it's him, but oh my god this feels so unnecessarily complicated. If that was intentional then I guess he did a good job but this is not a movie I care about. Maybe I'm just kinda dumb (very possible) but it was so hard to follow the names and who was doing what and whatever. I was hoping going in that the style would at least be engaging but there were no amazing montages or long flashy one takes that made me go "damn this is good". I was just confused all the time. And disturbed at the amount of feet. The acting was pretty good tho. Idk maybe this deserves a rewatch but this annoyed me so much wow. 

APV

Name: Some Kind of Heaven
Review:
Really enjoyed this! I love the hazy vibe here. This is truly surreal and dreamlike, at least as much as a documentary can be. I love the music and so many shots and transitions between shots and stuff like that. Unfortunately I don't think this was consistently visually on point enough to keep me totally interested. I love the concepts for each of the individuals focused on but this sort of dragged in the middle. I do love the melancholy created by the ending, though. Florida lol. If the story were more engaging I would love this a lot more, maybe I'll like it more when I'm older.

Hulu

Name: The Master
Review:
Masterful lol. After watching all of PTA's films, I understand better the distinction between his older and newer stuff, specifically in how his newer stuff is described as more mature and stripped back. Unlike Boogie Nights, Magnolia, or even PDL, this is NOT a flashy film. Paying full attention to this film was extremely rewarding. These performances are incredible, from the extreme physicality of Phoenix to the hilarious accuracy from Hoffman to the nuanced craze from Adams. I actually appreciated way more on a second watch the humor in Hoffman's performance. This shit is ridiculous, but also totally captures a certain vibe from good public speakers that have nothing to say (gave me high-school-history-teacher-to-be-a-football-coach vibes). This film is also beautifully shot. What brings this down personally for me is that the energy from There Will Be Blood and Boogie Nights is not quite there, and the emotional density from something like PDL or Phantom Thread isn't there either, which meant that I was not exhaustively effected in one way or the other like his other films. If you haven't seen any PTA, this is a bad place to start because it is less obvious what he is doing unless you watch his other more clear cut stuff. Still, great film, 4th best PTA.

Netflix

Name: There Will Be Blood
Review:
Stunned again. Totally lost for words. What a disturbing portrait of a man. 
Daniel Day-Lewis's performance here is almost certainly the best I have ever seen. This performance manages me to sympathize with a monster without making me feel bad for him at all, you know? This says so much through what it doesn't say, like there's a scene where in the background of the shot Plainview says something to Sunday but you don't know what it is. Also the way this film withholds information about Plainview's backstory keeps the audience at arms length from him just as he keeps everyone else, yet we still are able to piece together the sources of his disturbances. I also think the dynamics between Plainview and his son are so perfectly executed. If I'm not mistaken, every fading transition / audio splicing over a different shot goes from either work to work or family to work, emphasizing how Plainview's focus will inevitably turn from anything that isn't his work. There's also this shot of Plainview sitting with his son at a dinner table where the menu of the person at the other table is making a barrier between him and his son. 
I love so much about this, I'll save some of my thoughts for the next time I inevitably rewatch this. Probably my favorite PTA but I need to rewatch Phantom Thread again. 

Netflix

Name: Spencer
Review: Really liked this!!! I'll be honest I was kind of expecting this to be faster paced than it was. I was pretty bored through a lot of the first half (apart from some incredible scenes) because I don't think I was letting myself get immersed in the muted, castle-like prison created here. I was caught off guard by Stewart's performance too, but I think I got more used to it as the film went on. This is such a harrowing portrayal of being stuck. Paralyzed. This is a beautiful looking film, Stewart killed it, I just wasn't able to get super connected with the conflict and the characters. This is a subtle film and I didn't give it the respect it probably deserves so I'll rewatch at some point. Regal Theater Columbia

Name: We Need to Talk About Kevin
Review:
Sometimes I watch a movie and I'm like, damn, this director might make one of my favorite movies someday. While this isn't that movie, this had such incredible pieces. Presentation wise, I was surprised at how Lynchian some of the sequences felt. This film presents grief and regret in a very visceral, vibey way. The mood building here is phenomenal, from the score, to the out of place folky soundtrack (maybe connecting American music to this American story), to the way the camera lingers on the red of every shot. I'm also happy at how this didn't feel reductive in portraying why such events take place, a la "Elephant", though easily this film's weakest element is the portrayal of Kevin. Especially Erza Miller. He so badly wants to be the joker and it feels extremely inappropriate. I guess it might be accurate for a person in his situation to act in that way, except this doesn't seem to acknowledge that it's acting. Here, he's just the joker. It's pretty silly. That said, I do think it's interesting the chicken and the egg idea going on here with their relationship, especially considering this story is told from the mother's point of view. I also like how this is also a commentary on motherhood, in particular being a stay-at-home mother. This is almost "Elephant Man" except for the mother. I think this is much more interesting in that respect than the actual content surrounding this kid. So yeah, this is almost a masterpiece if it weren't for the silly portrayal of Kevin. I wish there were a movie about this topic that actually got one of these terrible people right instead of making the person a caricature. 

Hulu

Name: La Haine
Review:
This is probably severely underrating this film because honestly I wasn't super engaged in the beginning then I sort of checked out in the second half. I wish I would have given it my full attention, though, because this does discuss a lot of interesting topics in ways that -- get this -- are nuanced and not black or white.
