The style isn't as distinct and impressive as I have heard people describe it, but it was still pretty cool. Many interesting ways of showing what's happening on screen. Cassel is so impressive. I really need to see this again, but I loved it even though I was pretty distracted watching it. 

APV

Name: Swiss Army Man
Review:
I really enjoyed this! I love the premise and the philosophy of going about this story. This feels a little "Walter Mitty"-esque in how transcendental and V I B E Y this experience is with the music and the visuals, except that this film has a farting motorboat. I love the comedy here and the visuals most, but what drew this down for me a lot were 1) silly things that make no sense (things that weren't for comedic effect and were just lazy) and 2) the pacing (this really slowed down after the first 25 minutes but picked up again amazingly). I think these filmmakers have some great potential, this isn't quite it though. Still, highly recommend!

🏴‍☠️

Name: Paths of Glory
Review:
I wasn't completely engaged in the beginning, but by the end I couldn't look away. This explores themes of other Kubrick films on a smaller scale, most significantly regarding the inherent corrupt-ness of not just war, but the organization of the military. This movie pissed me off and almost made me cry. I don't think this is the most complex film ever made, but it's a powerful statement nonetheless. I especially loved the cinematography's ability to portray a feeling of inevitability. Check this one out, it's great.

APV

Name: Irreversible
Review:
First off -- do not rent this from Amazon. It stretched rather than preserved the aspect ratio. 

I think my rating is generous here. Noe as a filmmaker does not make "meaning" his number one goal. His films I have liked in the past, however, are a more refined version of his style, so that the experience of watching his film makes up for the lack of a great point to it. This, however, is full of decent visual ideas sprinkled with TERRIBLE story decisions. In Enter the Void, it made sense that I felt disturbed (as in, the disturbing stuff had an interesting formal explanation). Climax was meant to be a horror movie, but this just feels pretentious and gross. This is no meditation on fate, this just glosses over the idea, equivalent to reading the dictionary definition to the audience. This was uncomfortably disturbing, and the characters, concept, and visuals gave me nothing to latch on to. I might have liked this a lot more if this were my first of his, but this sort of sucks. Wouldn't recommend this, though I felt constantly engaged and it's still visually cool to watch a Noe film.

Tubi

Name: C'mon C'mon
Review:
This movie manages to be so comforting, warm, and real while also being so long and so boring. There are some incredible moments, this feels so genuine, the acting is great, and the writing is good (other than the kid feeling too much like an adult sometimes). This just feels a little pretentious and long. Still, happy I've watched, and the warm feeling this gave is something I want to see in more films. 

Ragtag Cinema

Name: West Side Story
Review:
I loved everything technical about this, but the story itself is just so not my thing. Romeo and Juliet as an original story is already questionable for me, even with the fact that it's actually satirical. Here though the stupidity is not addressed at all, this is some people die because of love at first sight bullshit and it makes no sense. Wack. I also thought the acting, other than from Riff, was inconsistent. 
That said, this film looks and sounds basically perfect. There are scenes where chaotic tension is developed through the overwhelming mix of the songs and it was super cool. I also thought the vast majority of the songs were really fun to watch (not as much the let's-just-stare-at-this-actor songs). HOLY MOLY the dancing was cool. This was a really fun watch, but man, how is this story considered so great? Get outta here 4.1 average rating this is overrated. 

AMC Chesterfield

Name: Whiplash
Review:
Yeah this contains one of the most interesting power dynamics I have ever seen in a film. This asks a question (what is worth sacrificing to be the best) and provides two interesting and complex answers to the question. I love how neither Andrew nor Fletcher are good people because it allows the audience to consider both sides (not to say they are equally bad people). I connected to this film from a personal level too being a part of a competitive music ensemble with a director that used similarly (WAY LESS EXTREME) manipulative tactics to allow for us to be good. 
Fletcher is one of my favorite characters ever written and performed. You, as the audience, are put so far away from him that it's impossible to know what he's doing or why he's doing it (making for an incredible rewatch). Best ending I've ever seen, hands down. That moment of silence is breathtaking. 

BluRay

Name: The Matrix Reloaded
Review:
There's a good movie in here somewhere...maybe? I think the ideas here are OUTSTANDING, but it is not well executed. There are some phenomenal fight scenes (though the original is still better, largely due to the more conservative CGI) and I think the performances were completely appropriate for the story. I also appreciate the attempt and discussing philosophical ideas such as determinism and free will and stuff, but oh my gosh its so ham-fisted. It's like an intro chapter of a philosophy book being shoved in your face. A great movie would discuss these concepts through the story/characters, not through poorly paced expositon dumps. Speaking of, the pacing is the worst thing. The first 40 minutes it feels like a poorly scripted podcast and the last 40 minutes are so jam packed with action it's kind of funny. I also thought this film could have been elevated by a better score. I love the aesthetic of the first film, but it feels like this one is trying too hard to escape the 90s-isms of it's predecessor. Take me back! Still though, the look, ideas, and incredible fight sequences made me enjoy myself just enough. 

Bluray

Name: The Matrix Revolutions
Review:
This is even more frustrating than the second one. The world building, philosophy, and atmosphere is completely wasted for this story. I enjoyed the first 20 minutes, but after that it was just boring conversation followed by mediocre action set piece. This film generally looks pretty cool and I am SURE the wachowski sisters could have done something better with the premise of the first movie, but the amount of dumb conversations are hilarious. This is such a roundabout way to talk about such a simple story. This (and the second matrix) are so inefficient, and this time I wasn't consistently entertained. And oh my god, NOW I know what people mean when they talk about the performances. They were so annoying here lol it was soooooo lifeless for no reason. I wish this story was done with a different background environment. 

Bluray

Name: Uncut Gems
Review:
Really loved this, but this doesn't quite hit the same heights as Good Time imo. This does a fantastic job creating a stressful environment with a thrilling story. I also really liked the look of the film -- a lot of light but a surprising lack of color, the opposite of Good Time. I kind of wish I saw this before because I can't help but think this is just a less potent version of it. I think another thing holding this back is that this character isn't quite likeable enough, but more importantly, I have no reason to care if he wins. Again, GT had the brother element to it that really made it more emotionally potent too. That said, damn, the Safdies are such exciting directors. I'm so happy the zaniness of Scorsese's After Hours lives on. 

Netflix

Name: The Power of the Dog
Review:
I've realized lately that some of my favorite movies have a conflict centered around the glacial movement of power between wildly different characters. This film really scratches that itch: these characters and their relationships with each other are unbelievably dense. Now, it takes a while for this film to reveal what it's really about, and that made it so that I was far more engaged the further the film progressed, but after it reveals itself I find myself wanting to go back to what I previously thought was boring. That's not to say there is nothing to look forward to until the end. The beginning's performances, camerawork, and score all contribute to a uniquely ominous vibe that carry on to the end, similarly overwhelming to something like Spencer (though I think this film capitalizes on that anxiety better). If you enjoy movies like Whiplash or Phantom Thread, I highly recommend this one. Just remember to keep an open mind until the end. I think you'll want to give it another watch. 

Netflix

Name: Hustlers
Review:
There is a better movie in here somewhere. This desperately needed like 20-30 minutes completely cut from the film, that shorter run time would compliment the flawless music choices and repetitive yet fun rhythm. My favorite parts of this movie are ultimately derivative -- the Wright-esque kinetic camera work and the Scorcesse-esque subject and plot are entertaining, but not as good as what they are imitating. I also found myself annoyed at it's messaging. This should be a film about what poverty does to people, yet the film makes multiple "crazy crackhead" jokes and contains multiple obnoxious examples of corporate sponsorships antithetical to the message of the film. Still, there were some creative shots and in general I loved the philosophy of a constantly moving camera, but by the end all I can think about is what this could have been. 

Hulu

Name: Dazed and Confused
Review:
Don't have too many strong feelings on this one. Watching a bunch of stupid young people is pretty fun, but it's also a bunch of stupid young people. Something something get off my lawn. These characters are pretty memorable and authentic, but I much prefer the Before trilogy to this. 

APV

Name: The Matrix Resurrections
Review:
This has the opposite problem to the other Matrix sequels: the abstract story is super interesting, but the action is soooo uninspired. I love what this film explores regarding reboot culture and the influence of the first film. I also love how this CHILLS OUT compared to the absurdly serious stakes of the two other sequels. This scope is purposefully small to compliment the greater thematic goals, which I really appreciate. Still, unfortunately, I was not particularly engaged for the majority of the runtime. I loved the montage in the beginning but that's the only thing that really WOWED me. I think I like this more than the second but I would rather watch that anyway. Dunno. Not what I expected for sure. 

Galleria 6

Name: Licorice Pizza
Review:
What a disappointment. Is this a poorly made movie? No, but what it attempted to do was both boring to watch and thematically really questionable. The only way this movie works is by viewing it as a satire on things socially acceptable during the 70s. If this is actually a story where I'm supposed to care about these characters, hooooo boy what a swing and a miss. This is well shot, but totally directionless plot and theme wise. PTA is so good at developing themes through stories of toxic relationships, but here what this film is trying to say is just so uninteresting and unsatisfying to me. Again, this isn't bad, this is just really REALLY not my thing (I've discovered I really dislike "hangout" movies unless it's the Before trilogy). I dunno, if you LOVE something like Dazed and Confused or Once Upon a Time in Hollywood maybe check this out (even though this is a worse version of those), but if you are going in as a PTA fan, prepare to be disappointed. 

Plaza Frontnac

Name: Taxi Driver
Review:
This is pretty much flawless. I love so much about this, but I think my favorite thing here is the grimy, colorful, and foreboding atmosphere. The music is tonally chaotic, switching up vibes extremely fast. This grime and gunk given to the audience portrays masterfully both what darkness this city contains as well as how Travis looks and feels from being in the city. Is this ominous vibe from the city itself, or is it just because we're looking at it from the eyes of someone who hates it? 

To say the atmosphere is my favorite part is massive praise considering how on point every other aspect of the filmmaking is. The acting from DeNiro is jaw dropping. This guy is so creepy and weird and you can just see the cogs turning by looking at him. The amount there is to analyze and talk about here rivals some of the best books I've ever read -- this is the movie that made me realize movies were a medium with the same analytic potential as a great novel (can we get films in English classes please, this is a great first pick). I mean, just LOOK at the moral grayness. JUST LOOK AT IT. 

Netflix

Name: The Raid
Review: One of my new favorite action films!! In one word, this movie is efficient. Every scene is either kickass or masterful at developing characters. This is some of the most creative and over-the-top action I have ever seen. That alone makes this worth a watch, but what really elevates this for me is the inclusion of such interesting characters and complicated moral dilemmas. This film takes full advantage of this story. Also, the music is fantastic and the cinematography is consistently interesting and compliments the action and suspense building so well. Unfortunately, this is the first movie I've seen where I noticed how ugly the film is from a color perspective. The whole film looks muted. This is probably intentional, but I still think more could be done to use this part of the medium. Still, fantastic action, highly recommend!! APV

Name: The Act of Killing
Review:
Watching this in the middle of the night during a bit of an emotional slump is a terrible idea. This is easily the most heartbreaking, mind melting film I have ever seen. This is Borat if he were Hitler and real. At first I was disturbed but not super engaged, but as the film ambled on the true evil penetrated my head and heart to the point where I'm just sitting on my couch with an indescribable feeling of disgust and disappointment. I don't know how this got made, but I wish there were more pieces of media so piercing. I've become pretty desensitized to disturbing stuff, and this broke through that shell. Probably won't ever watch this one again. Soul crushing.

Hulu

Name: The Godfather
Review:
Incredibly high highs, but I don't think the runtime is completely justified. I was not constantly engaged, though this is masterfully made, acted, shot, and (don't normally notice this) -- lit. Going agaikst this movie is the unfortunate truth that I can't tell the difference between characters in a film if there are more than like 8 so the Paulies and the Cawstellos kind of mix up for me and I'm just like wtf is going on (I suspect this is just a me issue). This is absolutely something to return to in the future. 

APV

Name: Gone Girl
Review:
This is so messed up oh my god LOL. This is evil I'm like laughing rn oh god it's so MESSED UP. I think this a perfectly cast movie, which is interesting considering the choices made here. I dunno, this was thrilling and crazy and wild but I don't think there's much above that. The stylistic choices are pretty minimal, almost reminded me of a Nolan flick in how plot focused it is. I get the hype due to how amazingly Fincher adapted this story to be an entertaining and creepy thriller, but this is still maybe my least favorite of the Finchers I've seen (other than Mank). Wish there was more to chew on thematically -- they sort of dabble in what truth means and the tendency not to believe men (which I'm not too sympathetic too, I feel like The Hunt did it better), but it just wasn't quite enough for me to LOVE love this. Still, happy I watched, and had a...good time? 

Hulu

Name: Taste of Cherry
Review:
I've been thinking about this throughout last night and this morning and I keep tapping into such a beautiful feeling. I'm not easily engaged by slow movies, and I am not going to pretend I wasn't bored before I had a better understanding of what this film was doing to me, but once this film clicked it CLICKED. This is a minimalist meditation on death, but I think it ends up revealing itself as beautiful examination on why people choose to keep going. The conversations Mr. Badii has with these people all provide unique insights into why people live, what suicide means, and the unique perspectives people pick up both through age and through different life experience. Homayoun Ershadi provides such a subtle yet breathtaking performance. Just thinking of certain scenes centering him creates a tidal wave of emotion even now. I also think that the style of Kiarostami is PERFECT for a film like this. The slowness and realism are exactly what's needed to hammer home the film's thesis, not to mention other unique tactics used more liberally for Kiarostami's other film Close Up ( a film I respected but did not connect to nearly as much ). Having watched some depressing shit recently, I did not expect this film to have the effect on me it did. I am so excited to watch this again and again. If you're looking for some slow, meditative, purposeful cinema, PLEASE check this out. 

HBO Max

Name: Mother
Review:
I was worried I wasn't going to love this considering how much I liked The Host, but MAN this is amazing!! This is such a thrilling mystery film with brilliant acting, a completely unpredictable story, and STUNNING camera work. I love the themes this film touches on and how slowly the true point of the film shows itself. My only minor critique is that this didn't seamlessly blend together the seriousness with humor like Parasite did. Still, it's a lot better than The Host in that way. Man, I'm in love with this film. If you want a Fincher itch scratched, watch this one. Also, this story is crazier than Parasite, so there's that. So many HOLY SHIT moments, at least 5 probably.

Hulu

Name: Moon
Review:
Super fun, medium-concept sci-fi with a likeable lead and brilliant story. I do wish this gave more to offer visually. I also think this concept could have gone a TAD further, but nonetheless I had a great time! Not too much to say other than that and that the music is F Y E

Hulu

Name: Don't Look Up
Review:
There's a scene here where DiCaprio's character has an impassioned rant about how he is sick of having to be fun and presentable when talking about the end of the world. I think it is the funniest thing that Adam McKay sat at the directors chair watching this rant, and then after filming finished said something like "Alright! Everyone in your places for the Sting farting scene!".

This problem is what brings this from a "meh" film to a frustrating one. Making a film about climate change as a comedy has the potential to be insensitive, and uh yeah this managed to do that. All this "satire" accomplishes is exaggeration -- a tactic fantastic for humor but terrible for changing minds. This is a film for rich white people to laugh at and feel good about liking. Everyone that wants to watch this movie already knows what this film is getting at. The question this film fails to answer is "what do we do?". I appreciate this film's courage in tackling such a dark subject, but what good is this going to do other than. I guess, make people laugh at it? 

This film REALLY needed some meta elements to it. Maybe the end could have been a family watching this film and seeing their ambivalence. Maybe the increasing absurdity of side characters as the film goes on could have been a meta  commentary about how the writers need to include silly elements in order for a film like this to get made. I don't know. Anchorman: The World's Natives Are Going To Die is just really not it.

I hope this is the worst Best Picture nomination. Please.

Name: CODA
Review:
Oh my god this is the corniest movie I have ever seen. The story is so silly and made me cringe more than once. Especially the choir stuff (the climax of her singing arc specifically made me wince my nose into my skull it was so sappy) . This film is so simple from a story and presentation perspective and relies almost completely on the audience developing a connection with these charismatic actors. I often felt bored; this movie did NOT need to be this long. Cut out the love interest entirely, it just felt like it was there because they thought it should be there. 

This film is so, SO lucky these actors gave the performances they did. Especially the father, he was electric. Even after all these critiques I do have to admit I literally almost cried. I've only cried to a movie like 3 times? THATS HOW SAPPY THIS IS I AM NOT JOKING. If you're looking for something simple that will make you feel something, this is a fantastic choice. Brain not required. Why is this nominated for Best Picture?

Apple TV

Name: Boys State
Review: This is a great idea for a documentary, but ultimately I don't think this completely takes advantage of its premise. I feel like a lot of energy went into making the political drama suspenseful, but nothing unexpected happened here. This went basically exactly how you think a convention of 17 year old boy aspiring politicians would go -- if that's the case, why create suspense? Emphasize the absurdity. There were some attempts to connect this to the current political landscape, but these connections are surface level and boring (the disconnect between politics and policy, the pitfalls of the party system, the efficacy of mudslinging, etc.) I also think this film really suffered from just covering one year. I think it would have been a much more interesting film to cover the history of the event. Get the perspective of former participants, some who continued into politics, some who changed course, people who run it, people who used to run it, etc. Maybe get different perspectives into what makes this event valuable (or NOT valuable). I don't know, I don't think the participants of this specific year were interesting enough to carry an entire film. There wasn't enough spice. I was not a fan of the look of the film (can't exactly place why), but I especially think the score was a strange choice. Sometimes the Boys State band, sometimes this Reznor-esque industrial stuff? Nah, keep it consistent.

Name: Memories of Murder
Review:
MY BOY BONG DOES IT AGAIN. 

I LOVE this -- being someone that enjoys crime films, it's cool to see how this must have inspired many other films to come after it (Prisoners and Zodiac are the most obvious I can think of). I think this movie reaches the heights of Parasite when it comes to the uniquely Bongian tone: this manages to walk that fine line between serious and light hearted so perfectly. I think this is also Bong's most dynamic film from a character perspective, it was so rewarding to pay attention to how these characters acted and felt throughout (again, this REALLY reminded me of Zodiac). I think I like this more than Mother due to how cleanly this film comes together thematically. I also think the atmosphere is so perfect, from the muted colors to the incredible music. I think the only thing preventing me from calling this perfect is the lack of emotional impact, though that's not to say that a rewatch won't yield a higher score. I HIGHLY recommend giving this a shot, maybe even as your first Bong hit.

Name: North by Northwest
Review:
This was very silly. I didn't know going in how much of a comedy this would be. A lot of it completely doesn't work and becomes funny by accident? Like, the dialogue is so 50's witty it 360s into funny. I think the plot idea is hilarious and thrilling and some sequences, mostly in the first half, were OUTSTANDING. This is well shot, the music is pretty great, but this did NOT need to be so long. These characters are so underdeveloped and so much screen time is devoted to their bantering and it justttt doesn't work. This film worked best for me when it was going for the suspense and made the audience's head spin at the spiderman-pointing meme of it all. I dunno, I get the classic status, but this is easily the worst Hitchcock I've seen so far. 

Also, wtf Hitchcock, why did you put your head in Mount Rushmore. Bro. 

HBO Max

Name: The Worst Person in the World
Review:
I don't normally like ambling, vibey, life movies like this. Going in this movie I was concerned I would be frustrated at a lack of a cohesive statement in favor of capturing an emotion (cough cough Licorice Pizza). To my surprise, this managed to be exactly what I was fearing and I LOVED IT. While I wasn't always on the edge of my seat, and the pacing is not perfect, I had such an amazing time. This is really about what it means to live for yourself: how it's beautiful and necessary, but also how it's scary or can make you feel like -- get this -- the worst person in the world. This is coming-of-age (whatever that age may be) done right. These characters are so complex and sympathetic and selfish and everything between. Trier conveys so masterfully a wonder for life without an ounce of insincerity or fake-ness. Yes, the movie kind of goes all over the place, but unlike other films of this genre I feel like all the episodes are thought provoking and necessary. This is also BEAUTIFULLY shot and scored, and when these technical aspects come together for certain artsier sequences, I cannot get enough. This isn't the kind of film that is easily put into words, I just have such a beautiful feeling after watching that I think you'll have too after viewing. Just trust me. 

Ragtag Cinema

Name: Drive My Car
Review:
I'm in the strange position of completely getting the people saying this is the best film of the year, 10/10, etc. , while also completely getting people saying this is the worst thing they've ever seen. I think this is certainly more good than bad, but it is absolutely not for everyone. 

This is a meditative, immersive, and perhaps extremely pretentious slowburn drama. There are parts of the execution I love -- the pretty camera work, the subtle acting, the courage to go into a topic leaving no corners untouched, and the last 45 minutes had me completely enchanted. Unfortunately, after leaving, I can say that a lot of the conversations either didn't stick, didn't make sense to me thematically, or maybe were just there for the v i b e s. Dunno. 

If you want the movie equivalent of a quiet 3-hour walk in a park (slow, kinda boring, but introspective as hell), this is the movie for you. I'm happy I watched it, but unless I really need that walk, I'll probably never see this one again. 

HBO Max

Name: The Batman
Review:
Do NOT go into this film expecting Se7en or Zodiac. While this is a dreary, brooding Batman mystery, unfortunately I feel like the hype around this film is directly connected to a Batman movie being pretty good. I love the atmosphere, there are some cool shots (not enough), and the performances here from many cast members is stellar, but the mystery wasn't mysterious enough, the artsy shots weren't artsy enough, and the gritty realism wasn't disturbing enough. Still, the Batman was certainly sad enough, and perhaps my favorite part of this movie. This guy is actually a psycho to be doing what he's doing, and this is the first Batman movie I've seen to acknowledge that. I love Pattinson in this movie and I hope he gets to take on this role again. Maybe not Matt Reeves next time though. 

Marcus Chesterfield Galaxy

Name: Nightmare Alley
Review:
To make a great film with a story like this (something you can really see from a mile a way), the visuals and overall presentation needs to be immersive and the characters need to be complex. I am most disappointed with how this film looks. I don't know why, but the entire time I was watching I only saw people living in the 2020s. These shots have no grit or gunk to help put me in this world. I really wish this had an atmosphere half as effective as something like "The Elephant Man", something that took place at the same time but really presented that eerie, creepy carnival vibe in a much more affecting way. 

Character wise, I feel like you can call the entire film and these people's arcs from the first 45 minutes. This doesn't provide any compelling commentary either. Still, this is a very competently made film. The acting is solid, the music is solid, and the story is fun enough to watch. I also think the sparse disturbing scenes here are very effective at packing a punch. I dunno, I just don't think this has quite enough going on to justify the runtime or even a best picture nom. 

HBO Max

Name: The King of Comedy
Review:
Maybe a bit overrated, but still loved it!! DeNiro really is one of the greatest. He plays creepy and slimy in so many different ways in so many films lol. This was a thrilling and often hilarious watch, and I love the unreliable narrator aspects here. I also think there is some interesting commentary on what it means to be famous/funny/"better"/etc. I wish the presentation was a bit more stylized and clearly Scorcesse, and I also think there could have been more done with these characters. Still, I'm glad I watched and I highly recommend!!

Hulu

Name: King Richard
Review:
This is such a bland film, unfortunately. This felt far too sanitary to make me believe this is how things happened. Were they really the most absurdly loving family of all time? Like, all five sisters constantly vibing with each other and bringing each other up? Jealous. The conflict here was really lacking. This kind of dabbled in talking about what it meant to be black going into tennis (not enough), about the scary potential of pushing these kids too hard (not enough), and what it takes to be a parent that wants their kid to be the best while also trying to protect them (not enough). There were some interesting strands and off-hand comments, but by the end it becomes pretty clear this film is just a Will-Smith-gets-an-Oscar vehicle. 

Also, what a wasted opportunity to make tennis in a film look cool. Tennis is so action-packed and intense, and none of the camera work did anything to highlight that. Sad. 

APV

Name: Fantastic Planet
Review:
Animation has such absurd potential to create a stunning visual experience. I don't think I've seen an animated feature that felt as understanding of this idea. In 90% of these scenes we get introduced to something that makes you say out loud "what the hell am I looking at?" This is some of the most immersive and imaginative sci-fi I have ever seen. The visuals and tone are the main selling point of this film, but the story is not half bad either -- a beautiful (even if sometimes rushed) tale about what it means to be "superior". The story gives room for interpretation while also clearly demonstrating a major fault that humanity has forever fallen into. If you want something trippy, but like in an adult Dr. Suess / Thomas the Tank engine way, this is going to be your new favorite thing. 

HBO Max

Name: Y Tu Mamá También
Review:
Something that sometimes bothers me about movies that attempt to provide a "slice of life" is how the movie may sometimes cut away from moments that are important to the characters only to avoid a harsher rating. This film does NOT have this problem. Every second the camera lingers feels proportional to how important these moments are to these characters, which means a lotttt of graphic sex. I love how focused this film is in making the experience of watching this movie as though you are actually with these characters, from the stuff that is explicitly focused on (the sex, the fighting, the fun conversations, etc.) while also subtly adding details to the background to emphasize how the setting may be suffocating everyone shown. 
This feels like a much better version of something like Dazed and Confused in how this captured youthfulness and wonder while also creatively portraying the setting as well as how that setting affects these people. Unfortunately, this film has a GLARING flaw: I really don't like these main characters at all. Moments I think we are supposed to be charmed and engulfed in the conversation seem to accidentally just gross me out. This would have been much better with more sympathetic main characters, in my opinion. I guess there is something to be said for the honesty of making these teenage boys so much like teenage boys, but if you're making a hangout movie you want me to enjoy, I've gotta kind of like them at least. Still, I love this film. Super excited to see Cuaron's other works.

Name: Belfast
Review:
I liked this more than I thought I would! This film made me feel like a kid and miss my grandparents. Visually this is pretty great, I just wish there were a bit more holding this narrative together. This seems like the sort of thing that you would LOVE if you grew up at this time/place, but unfortunately all I get out of this is a happiness at how nostalgic Branagh is here. I also loved one of the tracks, it gave me The Legend of Zelda: Spirit Tracks vibes??? I especially liked the father and grandfather's performances. I think many choices here were made to make you feel innocent watching what's happening around you, but that's just not compelling to me. I got bored especially in the middle. I dunno, this certainly wasn't bad, just really really not my thing. 

Ragtag Cinema

Name: The Truman Show
Review:
I watched this for the first time like six years ago and it became my favorite movie. I had TASTE as that 13 yo boy. 

This is such a fun concept executed in such a fun and engaging way. I love the attention to detail in the set design and cinematography -- especially on what the camera was focusing on at what times. I also adore how the social commentary is clearly there but also totally ignorable. This film manages to give such a great time, brain on OR brain off. 

Truman is literally one of my favorite characters in any movie ever. There's not a whole lot to him, but he's so innocent and emotionally exaggerated and hilarious that I rooted for him so hard from beginning to end. I'm not sure the emotional oomph would have been the same without Carrey in the lead. I literally almost cried (that doesn't happen very much). 

I dunno, I had such an amazing time watching this film. As much as I love it, this didn't quite reach its full potential, particularly in regards to the visuals of the film (or more specifically, the differences between Truman's world and the outside world). I also think the tone is not quite what I would have liked it to be. This sort of gives super-light-dark comedy vibes and I wish it delved deeper with the filmmaking into how harrowing this experience actually is (a la Groundhog Day).

Still, LOVED LOVED LOVED LOVED LOVED this. YES!

HBO Max

Name: The Virgin Suicides
Review:
This films biggest strength is being a vehicle for SC to flex her directing chops. I LOVED the creepy atmosphere, experimental visuals (though some have not aged fantastically), muted color palette, and haunting soundtrack. This film has what many 10/10 films have. I am extremely excited to check out her other work. 

Unfortunately, the story and acting severely set this film back. The thematic purpose is conveyed in an irresponsibly vague way. It is NOT clear enough that the boys and the community are the true villains and the main focus of the story. Treating this subject matter with such vibiness accidentally only serves to romanticize suicide -- something that could have only happened through mishandling the source material. It makes me sad to read these reviews talking about how this is a beautiful portrait of depression. THAT'S NOT THE POINT, and for the film to not emphasize this is extremely irresponsible. I dunno, I really enjoyed myself but I left feeling pretty hollow and with a bad taste in my mouth. 

APV

Name: The Handmaiden
Review:
WHAT THE HELL IS THIS AHAHHAHAHAHAH WHAT

This is, no exaggeration, the wildest and least predictable movie I have ever seen. Speechless. Seriously -- what? This film rewards paying attention and asking questions. For much of the ride there were errant details that ended up paying off MASSIVELY. As the film progresses, it becomes clearer and clearer how skilled these actors are. While I think it's certainly a CRAZY experience to go into this blind, I'm almost positive I will rate this higher on a rewatch. This film is genuinely beautiful from a technical perspective, but honestly what really makes this a near masterpiece is the pacing, the delivering of information, and the ability here to make you scream at the TV. I think (other than, uh, you'll know when you see it...and keep seeing it) this is, like, the perfect argument against being okay with "decent" movies. This has the thrill and chaos and kinetic energy marvel and Nolan fans claim to love. WATCH OTHER STUFF WHY ARE YOU DOING THIS TO YOURSELF I DO NOT UNDERSTAND. This is the kind of film to make me upset at other films. Especially structurally. You'll know. 

I really wanted to give this a 10 but I'm just not sure this created quite a powerful enough thematic/emotional catharsis to me to warrant that. Now that I know what's going on, it might be different for the second watch. 

PLEASE WATCH THIS OMG

APV

Name: Mad Max: Fury Road
Review:
What a time efficient and bonkers action movie! I think it's interesting how what makes this film good is so different from what makes the other ones good. This has a surprising amount of goofiness, but the unashamed maximalism of these set pieces really allows for this kind of absurd goof to shine. This is easily the least dark of the franchise, but perhaps the most detailed in its world building. This film focuses heavily on giving a weird ass post-apocalyptic experience with non-stop INSANE action, and from that perspective, this is a masterpiece. 

I also think these character choices are impressively subversive, especially surrounding gender roles in film. This didn't go how I was expecting in a great way.

This is pure popcorn fun. Watch this if you want to immerse yourself in some strange world building with hilariously intricate and well-coordinated action. 

Blu-ray

Name: First Reformed
Review:
This is the kind of movie to slowly gnaw at you long after watching. This feels like a minimalist, more existential Taxi Driver. The camera rarely moves, the colors are muted and dull, and the score creeps up behind you when you least expect it, all antithetical choices to its spiritual predecessor. These choices stunt the viewer's ability to get distracted, which creates an ascetic experience where all there is to immerse in is Toller's encroaching nihilism.

I love how this movie frames climate change. I think this portrait of it is going to age unfortunately well -- especially regarding the politics conversation (THIS is how you do it, "Don't Look Up"). 

Hawke delivers such a sad, restrained performance. There are scenes where he gets mad, and I LOVE how he expresses that anger, it's perfect to how this character would act. I also think this does a better job than Taxi Driver at making the lead sympathetic, though perhaps at the cost of some moral dubiousness.

There are some stylistic choices that at first might seem out of place or random, but I think the ending justifies all these strange decisions so that it doesn't become as out of left field. I love how this ending allows the audience to make a decision at how to look at the film, and how there is evidence for many readings. I love it when films do that.

I don't know, this really surprised me. Right now my biggest critique is how after a certain point I knew what would happen due to Taxi Driver, but this is such a wildly different way of going about the presentation and themes of the story that it might bother me less on a second watch. This is one of the best films I've seen so far this year, just make sure you are emotionally ready for this one.

APV

Name: My Neighbor Totoro
Review:
The most wholesome and happy movie I think I've ever seen. Perfect for after something harrowing

HBO Max

Name: Lucky Number Slevin
Review:
Such a great time. This isn't anything ground breaking -- the story is unnecessarily convoluted as well as obvious, but Harnett as a lead is so fun. He carried this HARD. I also think some of the dialogue is trying so hard to be clever and falls flat on its face, but I had a fun time anyway!! This also pretty cleverly sets up ideas through minor details. I also appreciate the perspective choice and the presentation of information. The chemistry between Harnett and Lucy Liu is fun to watch as well. 

I have to say, though, some of the filmmaking choices are pretty annoying. There is an extended flashback scene at, like, half the frames, and it looked terrible. I also thought the casting of Freeman and Kingsley was so, so weird. Still though, if you want something that will keep your attention that you don't want to think about, this is a consistently entertaining and fun choice. 

🏴‍☠️

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review:
NEW FAVORITE ALERT NEW FAVORITE ALERT NEW FAVORITE ALERT.

Going in I was a little unsure -- is this really the BEST FILM OF ALL TIME like letterboxd number told me it was ?? 

The answer? Yeah basically. This is one of the best MOVIES I have ever seen. Cinema? Get outta here. 

This movie captures the energy of playing "pretend" as a kid -- at any moment, it feels like any new crazy, fun, weird stuff can happen. And it keeps topping itself scene after scene after scene. This film is such a sensory overload for like every emotion? How?

This is one of the most inventive, boundary pushing films I've seen from the last 20 years, easy. I've never seen anything so maximalist in its ethos yet so thorough in its execution. This concept has so much potential and I think, miraculously, Daniels squeezed out even more from it than I thought possible. Not only from a thematic and emotional way, but even from a FUN way (the process of gaining skills from another universe, the skills gained, the, uh, award, etc.).

I don't want to give too much away, but what this story actually reveals itself to be about is SO COOL. I'm happy this didn't go the way of The Matrix in creating something to be interpreted -- this has a pretty clear message, outstandingly executed. (googlies > baked good)

After watching I can totally understand how this became the highest rated movie on letterboxd. This has both quality AND quantity. There's complex, well-coordinated action, consistently creative comedy, philosophical commentary on nihilism, the proudest and biggest heart I've seen on film, and, somehow, more. The mastery of so many wildly different fun-time-moviemaking skills allows for such a high likelihood of people having a good time. Even if you're not as big of a fan of, say, the action, surely the comedy will keep you engaged, etc.

These characters are well developed and entertaining to watch. Ke Huy Quan is SUCH a standout. He had the opportunity to show off many different things, all of which were so fun to watch. There's an extended sequence with him in the end that literally made me cry it was so good. Michelle Yeoh also totally killed it, as well as the rest of the cast. There's so much earnestness in this story, and everyone showed up big time in creating that environment.

I literally haven't been as excited about the future and scope of film since I first watched Taxi Driver. I really hope other films follow this one's lead in the it's-a-movie-so-lets-let-ACTUALLY-crazy-shit-happen way. 

I really hope this is film blows up. This combines the world-building of The Matrix with the unashamed emotional vulnerability of something like Magnolia. And, sorry, is way better. PLEASE GO WATCH THIS PLEASE BUY A TICKET PLEASE PLEASE PLEASE

Ragtag Cinema

Name: Akira
Review:
This is GREAT! My criticisms are possibly as a result of watching such a complicated, unexplained story only once, but they still caused problems -- I did not connect with these characters, I felt that some of the action/adventure aspects were kind of boring imo, and god DAMN is this hard to follow. 

Still though, the last 20 minutes might be in my top 10-20 sequences in a movie EVER. This is also a rare movie that had me noticing how outstanding the sound is. The score is also literally one of my favorites ever -- it perfectly captures the dystopian unease this movie soaks with. Also, from what I could grasp at, what I THINK this movie is about is so so so so cool. This is something I will 100% return to so that I can have a more complete read on it, but this has a great chance at becoming a 5 later on. 

If you want to see the boundaries of animation stretched, this is a great pick. Be prepared to be slightly confused!

Hulu

Name: Blow Out
Review:
This movie went off the rails oh my god LOL.

I feel like people are rating what they want this movie to be rather than what it actually is -- I love the IDEA of a lowly sound guy becoming a detective unearthing a conspiracy, but the acting really holds this back. Travolta is okay, but Nancy Allen is BAD. There aren't any characters that aren't total cliches other than the two leads, and these are not two people you want to center a movie around. A lot of this is dialogue that doesn't work. I do appreciate the slow descent into paranoia and obsession, but the story and acting don't quite take full advantage of this potentially ripe detail.

My favorite part of this EASILY is De Palma's style. I love how much effort is put into making things visually interesting. I think a scene where the camera is spinning around in a circle is masterful, along with the general sequence where Travolta witnesses the crash. i don't think every experiment works (green screen weirdness), but I still had a lot of fun watching him try crazy stuff. 

I also think this film is genuinely hilarious, both intentionally and unintentionally. There are some great joke setups and payoffs, and I really enjoyed the Halloween homage fakeout thing in the beginning. I also couldn't help but burst out laughing when the credits rolled -- what the hell are those last 20 minutes?? 

I also think the writing in general is actually pretty good. I feel like there wasn't very much time wasted, things that were more drawn out ended up being important later. The slow revealing of information is also very satisfying.

I think it was possible for me to like this crazy mess a little more if this had a better score. The music is laaaaaaaaaaaaame. I was immersed in how much I loved the visuals and sound design, but oh my god this would have benefitted from a score as good as "Thief" or something. Also, I wish this were a little less like Chinatown, because I don't know why I would ever watch this over Chinatown (well, maybe I can think of one reason...)

Coming out of this, I don't regret watching it at all, I just don't think this is the De Palma for me. I think this guy could make one of my favorites, though!

APV

Name: Donnie Darko
Review:
A perfect example of substance-over-style.

This could have been a masterpiece under a different director. The story and script are mind-bogglingly intentional. I love how information is presented in a subtle way so that the audience slowly realizes stuff without things being annoyingly explained. Every scene moves the audience closer and closer to understanding what's happening in a satisfying way, and after watching the entire film I find myself wanting to watch it again to try to catch more stuff.

HOWEVER, this does not work for me at all from a stylistic perspective. The camera is claustrophobic in many scenes for seemingly no reason, the color palette is ugly, and some decisions regarding Gyllenhaal's performance really didn't work for me (why is every mentally disturbed character in film just the joker I hate it). Imagine being served an A5 Wagyu steak, except that the steak is raw and also the server dropped it just before getting to the table. Like, it's good??? But I'm not too appetized right now?? 

I think Lynch would have made a film on par with Mulholland Drive if he was given this script, but Kelley doesn't quite know how to make something engaging mentally while also being engaging in other ways too. In fact, this movie gives me even more respect for films like Primer and Upstream Color: these movies are more or less ABOUT trying to "solve" them, but the music, cinematography, and characters all make the experience enjoyable OUTSIDE of a gimmick. 

I really do get the appeal, but this is so far from being my thing. I think this is actually worthy of a remake from a more stylistic director. 

HBO Max

Name: Captain Fantastic
Review:
The last 20 minutes are the dumbest way this possibly could have ended oh my god. Viggo did great and I loved the music, but this just feels like a granola King Richard with an even more morally dubious "hero". Higher highs and lower lows than King Richard, so the same score seems reasonable. 

YT

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review:
I teared up a bit the first time but omg I basically sobbed this time. This is one of the most emotionally affecting films I've ever experienced, and somehow that only got stronger the second time watching. 

This film has the most range out of any film I've ever seen. Imagine a spectrum where one end is fart jokes and the other end is Nietzsche. This film is everywhere on that spectrum. I've never seen a movie so seamlessly combine antithetical tones. I can't stress this enough -- this contains a contender for the best comedic scene in film AND quite a few for most emotionally potent.  

I feel like for a lot of people there's a tendency to retract from corny, over-the-top sentimentality (myself included). It can often feel naive. There is a lot wrong with life in general, and to do something like say "I love you" or to put googly eyes on stuff can feel "wrong" in some headspaces, at least for me. This film is a meditation on this phenomenon. The film looks at this tendency and somehow, by the end, manages to cut through. This film is so aggressively, unashamedly goofy and saccharine, and that's something I think we need to consume more of. 

I dunno, this really is one of the best films I've ever seen, both as a comedy and as a family drama. I think I might have liked it more the second time. This is a really special movie to me and I'm prolllllllly seeing this again. I'm so lucky to be able to see this in theaters!!! SEE IT

Marcus Chesterfield

Name: The Player
Review:
This is one of the funniest not-laugh-out-loud comedies I've ever seen. 

Somehow this is able to make fun of the absurdity of Hollywood films and filmmaking while also making something that fits the mold. The story beats are ridiculous and unrealistic, but it TOTALLY works. This actually reminds me a lot of American Psycho in comedic tone, except this is much more fun and less serious. 

The acting, especially from Tim Robbins, is so perfect LOL. It's such a joy to watch, especially as the film progresses and the story gets more stressful. 

I also think from this movie I have to say that Thomas Newman is one of my favorite film composers. This is perfectly noir-y and mysterious while also fitting the tone this ultimately goes for. 

This manages to both be a fun, hilarious time while also sneaking in some absurd yet valid social commentary about movie making for the masses. If you want a fun meta mystery, check this out. SUPER excited for other Altman.

Name: mother!
Review:
This is OUTSTANDING. I think my viewing experience greatly benefitted from kind of knowing what this movie is about, so I didn't have the "wtf is going on" and being bored experience. 

I was expecting to be on board with this film because of the directing, but really I feel like the writing is what MAKES this film. This is a proper retelling of creation, except from the point of view of "Mother Earth". And this film sticks HARD with that POV -- everything we see is how she experiences it, and that really adds to the commentary here.

There are all sorts of arguments throughout time trying to reconcile how God can be the way he is while also creating the hellish world we live in, but I think the doors the audience is led down philosophically through so firmly putting the audience in this point of view is extremely interesting. This film got me asking the question: what differentiates God's creations?  Why are the inanimate so spiritually and religiously removed from the animate? All is God's creation -- how can one creation be worth more than another, especially considering our potential for destruction? 

I think questions like these and more are extremely interesting, and the seemingly slow pace actually often lends itself to making these questions more well developed. 

Now, while I love the philosophical directions, I do also love the visual style here. While for a while I wasn't super into it because of how samey everything felt (close, shaky shots of Lawrence and whatever she's looking at), but as these philosophical ideas came from these small retellings of Bible stories, I realized how purposeful and complementary this style is to conveying its themes. 

Is this for everyone? Probably not. I think this is great for people who are interested a deconstruction of the creation myth with extremely stressful filmmaking. 

Now, I don't think this is totally perfect. I wish Aronofsky pushed himself a little further with the style. I appreciate how held back and purposeful this was, but this is a WILD story, and I think it deserves a heavier directing hand. I'm also not entirely sold on Lawrence's performance. I think I get the choice, but some of the line delivery felt strangely unemphasized, not weak like I think the intension was. 

I love this director. I had a bad (good?) time!!!

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review:
It's taken me the third watch to really appreciate how outstanding the acting is. I liked her at first, but I REALLY love Yeoh's performance, actually. I tried to pay attention to it, and she plays it so perfectly. Her comedic timing is amazing, but she also displays wonder and despair and love so earnestly and fully, it's completely perfect. 

This film is still overwhelming even on a third watch. I caught some gags I didn't notice the first couple times and I was able to appreciate even more a lot of the joke/theme foreshadowing. 

This feels like a dramatic movie event, like what I would imagine The Matrix or Pulp Fiction or Forrest Gump to be like seeing it in a theater. I feel like this could be the kind of movie to lose some of its sheen as time goes on because of how copied I'm sure this will be (again, like the Matrix or Pulp Fiction). I'm glad to see it with both the emotional weight as well as the AWE of I CANNOT BELIEVE THIS EXISTS. Watch it pwitty pwitty please :( 

Ragtag

Name: Investigation of a Citizen Above Suspicion
Review:
My experience watching this was hurt by 1) being extremely tired and 2) the subtitles on amazon prime video are 5 seconds behind so I had to rewind multiple scenes to try to figure out who said what. I was literally just watching random people speak Italian for like 5 seconds before any words showed up.

BUT this was really really good. This cinematography had the same kinetic energy as something like "Point Break" actually??? Wasn't expecting that. There are so many long, windy, drawn out shots that were staggeringly cool looking. 

I think the social commentary is HILARIOUS, and I wish I knew going in that this is intended to be a sort of comedy. The climax of this film is both extremely funny but also strikes an outstanding point about the flaws of interrogation. 

This is a bold joke against the police, and from that perspective I really appreciate it. Now, I do think this was a little long, and the hilarious cartoon goofy bouncy noises in the score were distracting BEYOND comprehension (literally the score randomly goes BOIINNNGNGGG like a loony toon on a pogo stick or something it was wild) 

The film "La Haine" now has a new friend on the "watch it again when you get the criterion because I wasn't paying attention the first time" list. Good times! 

APV -- do not watch this here, the subtitles are god awful.

Name: The Northman
Review:
This film's biggest problem is that it is being pulled in two directions: the gnarly, trippy, and visceral way from Eggers, and the boringly epic, celebrity splattered, clean way from this film's funders. 

I loved a lot here, especially the elements relating to folklore and mysticism. I love seeing these character's feelings through spirituality; it's one of the ways film in general is cooler than real life. All visions, trances, and sorcerery here consistently blew my mind. 

Unfortunately, this gets watered down by the length. The atmosphere is nowhere near as immersive as "The Lighthouse" or as nihilistic as "The VVitch". I did not feel like I was watching something 1000 years old. I would not be surprised if the studio prevented Eggers from making this the non-English, inappropriately colorful film it should have been. This felt strangely modern, which put this film in an uncanny valley between "The Lighthouse" and "Clash of the Titans" or something. 

Still, this film has some outstanding moments. Eggers' vision still mostly pulled through and he made a wonderfully strange, kinda gross, kinda trippy big budget revenge film. Please let there be a directors cut. 

Ragtag -- also omg my theater was so annoying. I know it's Willem Dafoe, you don't need to tell the whole theater.

Name: Petite Maman
Review:
This is such a strange follow-up to something as GRAND as "Portrait of a Lady On Fire", but I still had a cute ol' time!! This film sacrifices surprises and distinctness in order to create something mellow, calming, and adorable. I like a lot of the editing choices, and I especially love how the less-is-more presentation portrays the relationships between each family member. 

Unfortunately, this didn't go much of anywhere. This is an accurate slice-of-life illustration of childhood, but there isn't much to this otherwise.  Maybe watch "My Neighbor Totoro" or "Coraline" first. 

Ragtag

Name: The Handmaiden
Review:
Yeah, after a rewatch, I think this is literally a flawless movie. This story has so many intertwined, moving parts, and somehow this film balances them all perfectly. I feel like this plot alone told conventionally would make an outstanding movie, but the decisions regarding point of view and carefully withholding information supplement an already outstanding story PERFECTLY. Basically, this is an outstanding plot TOLD in the perfect way. How often do you get both? 

This manages to ride the line between fun and thrilling while also being so artful in its cinematography and presentation. This feels like the perfect accessible movie in how twisty and turny it is while also firing on all other "film" cylinders. 

This is perhaps the wildest ride I've ever had watching a movie, even on a second watch. Being able to catch details and understanding these scenes under the context of having already seen them is unbelievably fun. 

This is one of the best films I've seen this year. I recommend this to anyone FUN. WATCH THIS! I know I will again.

Name: Ponyo
Review:
Easily the worst Miyazaki I've seen so far. I love a lot of the ideas here, but  this falls flat in a lot of the things I love most about his films. I think the animation is a little disappointing -- this is nowhere near as magical or as technically staggering as "Spirited Away". Many of the drawings seem more like story board art, in a bad way. I also think the world building is limited and underdeveloped. It was really cool looking, but nothing as wonder-filled as other works. 

Thematically and emotionally I don't feel like there's much interesting here. I also think the story, ESPECIALLY the ending, is not very good. 

All that said, this is still an adorable and fun film with a great sense of humor. I had a good time watching it, it's just not anything very special. 

Also, the mother is such a sociopath it's not even funny. 

Marcus Chesterfield

Name: Eyes Without a Face
Review:
I understand that looking at this film in isolation is extremely unfair due to how influential and groundbreaking this is, but there are plenty of old films that are influential and groundbreaking that actually hold up in 2022 (Citizen Kane, 12 Angry Men, etc.) This just didn't. 

There were two scenes where I was impressed or disturbed. Everything else felt like boring fluff. The score felt inappropriate. I don't think these characters are that interesting. I kind of understand how people could extrapolate interesting conversations about these characters moral ambiguity, but to me this feels black and white in a boring way. Yeah, the doctor feels guilt, but he's also just completely awful. 

I struggled to pay attention. If it weren't for the surgery scenes and the mid-film reveal this would be getting a much lower score. If you have never seen a horror film in your life and you want to see all horror movies from beginning to end, this is a great place to start. Otherwise, watch something else. 

HBO Max

Name: Groundhog Day
Review:
I think a lot of what I love about this movie honestly comes from the premise and how thoroughly it explores it. This is a fun, occasionally wild watch that's even more fun to think and talk about. 

This script (ALMOST ENTIRELY from a plot perspective) carries this film so hard. I wish this went further with its psychological horror aspects, because that is the true gold of this film. As a comedy, it's just alright.

I feel like "Everything Everywhere All At Once" could NOT have been made without this film, but I also think it makes this obsolete in some ways. That film is more thorough with its premise, has a more thoughtful exploration of nihilism, and has far more memorable characters, and is just a lot funnier. Still though, I'm happy this exists. (how stereotypical can I be with this paragraph lmfao) 

Also, Bill Murray. That is all. 

APV

Name: Apocalypse Now
Review:
NEW FAVORITE MOVIE OF ALL TIME.

I don't know why anyone would ever make a war film after this one. This is perfect. This film has the most impressive visuals of all time. I cannot believe the 1979 release date -- the "action" set pieces manage to be timeless in their terror. 

Every out-there stylistic decision lands. Every single one. From the layered shots, to the constant colored clouds, to the increasingly hectic lighting, I cannot get enough of this film's style. This combined with the transcendental score put me in a trance unlike any other film I've ever seen. 

This explores the "horror" of war so thoroughly through atmosphere and mood. This, out of any war movie I've ever seen, manages to immerse me  deepest in the jungle, the death, and the meaninglessness. This film is affecting in a way only film can. 

Prepare for an experience.

APV

Name: The White Ribbon
Review:
I respect Haneke's vision here, but this is the first of his films where his "I hate the audience" schtick felt more annoying than profound. Throughout my watch I waited for potential themes bubbling under the surface to rear their head, but the film's thematic depth exhausts itself in its clinical, humdrum first 30 minutes. 

This looks to me like Haneke's bleak, hopeless version of "Magnolia". This is Haneke at his most broad and shallow, attempting to accomplish something antithetical to his biggest strengths. This attempts to introduce far too many characters then are deserved or necessary to make his statement. A vast portion of the runtime is devoted to something, making the audience focus on it, then the film ends with the condescending question of "why did you care about this? Look at this other, more profound thing!? Why did you ever care about this other thing??" This tactic did not work for me at all because of how much I cared about BOTH things. What he implied to be ignored was so obvious and pervasive that pulling the rug out from under me makes me feel like I'm getting a stern talking to about something I already know. I DO put the toilet seat down, you don't need to convince me that I should! 

All that said, this film is still bloated with skill. While not to my tastes, the cinematography is impressive. Every shot feels like an old photograph. I also love what this film is trying to say, even if I didn't like HOW it was said. The pervasiveness of abuse and arrested emotional development is powerfully communicated. I also CANNOT get over how Haneke's twists of tragedy work so well with such minimalistic filmmaking. Every peer through a door creates such suspense without the aid of music or anything flashy. 

This is a well made film, but I saw through Haneke's tricks this time. I wish this film were less obvious, then he would be cooking with gas. Watch his other stuff first. 

APV

Name: All About Eve
Review:
This is the sort of film I would need context to give an intelligent rating to. As it stands, it's way too long, corny, and clichéd. But, I THINK the cliché of the story is intentional (just look at the critic character oh my god LOL).
 
I don't know, if this were a satire of Hollywood not only through the plot but through the presentation, this is a 4. If this is ONLY meant to be taken on the surface as an exploration of female identity and the way Hollywood churns out talent with no care for the talent as actual people, I think this has aged kind of poorly. 

Normally I read other reviews and I get these answers, but I genuinely cannot tell what I'm supposed to take away here. I'll hedge my bets and go in the middle. 

APV

Name: The Salesman
Review:
I hear people talking about the writing a lot, but MAN the direction sold this for me over everything else. I love how this encourages paying attention with how it establishes rhythm and breaks it for things to pay attention to. I love how tension is built through the mundane, almost like a Lynch or Haneke. There's a scene which lingers on a door that's unbelievably creepy. 

This is a slow burn, which did turn me off from the first 20-30 minutes of the film, but even then I couldn't stop gawking at how magnificently beautiful this film looks! Thankfully things really picked up to an outstanding climax that made me feel conflicted and uncomfortable...in a good way! 

I also appreciate how dark this manages to be without any disturbing imagery. I wonder if this has to do with censorship. Regardless, I think it served the movie in creating discomfort and pain through the results rather than the causes. 

This film meanders a teensy bit, and I'm not sure I totally got the Death of a Salesmen parallels, but when this gets going it gets GOING. If you're interested in moral dilemmas or outstanding cinematography (the mirror shot and the sound/lighting room shots, WOW), you need to check this out. I'm so hyped to check out Farhadi's other stuff.

Name: Breathless
Review: This is such a cool watch for me because this feels like the birth of so many things I love about more modern movies. This attempts to break so many arbitrary rules about cinema, and a lot of what's done ends up influencing not only film, but other forms of visual media. This shit is like 2011 YouTube with its jumpcuts lol. It's more than just form for me, though. I was genuinely interested in these characters and their motivations. This felt consistently thrilling seeing someone on the run the whole film. Is this emotionally impactful? Not at all -- this is almost a proto-Run Lola Run in what makes it appealing. Not so much a crazy emotional experience and more like an entertaining, visually subversive one. LOVED THIS! HBO Max

Name: Life Itself
Review:
This documentary attempts to be two things at once: a celebration of Roger Ebert's life; and a depiction of a good man's last unhealthy months. I get they weren't anticipating Ebert to die in the middle of making the film, but these two goals conflicted with each other to make a sometimes jarring, unorganized, and tasteless film. 

The director clearly sees Ebert as a hero, which is fine, but it makes for an uninteresting documentary, ESPECIALLY during such a time where criticizing Ebert would be disrespectful. Half of this movie felt like it could have been about any well-loved person dying. These parts of the film are sometimes powerful, haunting, and even inspiring due to Ebert's relentless optimism, but unfortunately indistinct. It's very easy to make me sad about watching this man slowly pass away because of how cool of a dude he was, but how much of that warrants praising this FILM? 

These parts of the movie can be moving, but they also made me uncomfortable. Especially in Ebert's last days, the director's questions really rubbed me the wrong way. It seemed like he was only communicating with Ebert for the sake of the documentary, and expecting Ebert in his pain to spend his literal last hours answering questions for this documentary. 

In short, this film was bloated with scenes that made me uncomfortable. I wish his family had more privacy during his death. 

Due to these circumstances, I feel like there was less time devoted toward the content of his work, his influence, and what it means to be a reviewer. I can't help but feel that Ebert would have preferred emphasizing these aspects. While many topics were covered, few felt like they were given enough time to blossom into interesting conversations. I did like the sections on Siskel and Ebert's tumultuous relationship, Ebert platforming unknown filmmakers, and seeing Ebert's reviews spliced with footage from the films. 

I wish this film were made either five years earlier, five years later, or just doubled down on Ebert's final days rather than attempting to cover both his life and his passing. 

APV

Name: The Red Shoes
Review:
EVERY SINGLE MOVIE FROM THE 40s HITS SO HARD 

This is one of the most stunning looking films I've ever seen. The colors are so vibrant and restless, like they're happy to be there. This whole film gives a fairytale storybook vibe, which gives me such a nostalgic and warm feeling -- really strange considering how dark the core story is. 

I think the performance by Anton Walbrook MAKES this film. He's enigmatic, only giving you what you can discern from his cold, stern face. I think the choice to not give him any sort of background other than being a successful, though perhaps morally dubious director is perfect. I had a lot of fun discussing his character and what his deal was. Why are you sitting in dark rooms alone, man, you good? 

I don't think it's clear at the beginning who this movie is really about, but by the end all of my qualms about lack of character development evaporate. We are given exactly what we need. 

This film could have been perfect if not for the last 20 minutes. This needed an extra 15 minutes to do everything it wanted. The ending is way too rushed and, at times, hilariously stupid. I like the idea, but the execution left a lot to be desired. 

Still, this is a thrilling, BEAUTIFUL, and complex work, one of the best I've ever seen. I think if the pacing were just slightly smoother and if this had a slightly better ending it would be perfect. This is a brilliant proto-Whiplash/The Piano Teacher. If you want a complicated character study that's also a visual masterpiece, check this out asap. Don't be scared by the year. 

HBO Max

Name: Lost in Translation
Review:
Yikes. So, Sofia Coppola now has a seat firmly planted next to M. Night Shyamalan on the "Directors I Wish Made Good Films" list.

Even after TWO lackluster, socially irresponsible, meandering duds, I'm STILL interested in seeing her other films due to how much I adore her style. The music, the hazy visuals, the detached characters, all of these aspects to her films are so interesting. But ohhhhh my god she wastes her talent on rich-people-being-sad cinema. 

Other than Coppola's visual direction, the only thing saving this movie is Scarlett Johansson's outstanding performance. She plays a young, insecure, nervous woman so eerily well. This reminds me of conversations I sometimes see between an older male teacher and a female student. She has everything to the excessive laughter DOWN. 

Unfortunately, this film otherwise is socially irresponsible and boring. I get that the intention is to show these people detached from life and their society, but the method of putting them in a foreign country and being weirded out by the culture and language, a country who's people have experienced life-altering racism from Americans, is so tasteless. There is an uncomfortable amount of humor surrounding how funny Japanese people sound and how weird their culture is, and it's like, really? Couldn't you have done, like, Germany or something? 

Even without this I was so bored. I don't know how I'm supposed to feel any sympathy toward these people, especially Bill Murray's character. This film does not acknowledge any immorality from these people. Instead, we're supposed to feel emotionally attached and sad at the ending. Why? They (mostly Murray) suck. 

I also hate how this film presents the idea that people experiencing these kinds of crises don't have anyone that will listen to them. This is such an unhealthy idea. NEITHER of the leads do remotely enough in their relationship to express that they are feeling the way they feel. Take responsibility for your relationship -- maybe don't cheat? Maybe express to them you feel bad? Brooding in your hotel gives me zero sympathy for you. 

This movie pissed me off. Murray's natural charisma, Johansson's career catapulting performance, and Coppola's visual style all keep this film from a lower rating, but no, I will never see this again. Watch something else. 

APV

Name: L.A. Confidential
Review:
I feel like I have a curse for blind buying blu rays... 

This is fine. I think I made the mistake of watching almost every critically acclaimed movie of this genre before seeing this one. I feel like there are so many movies that do what this did much better. Complicated crime story? The Handmaiden is better. Twists and turns? The Departed is better. Social commentary about the morality of justice? Prisoners is better. A lighthearted, fun time? Fargo is better. 

I understand that this isn't going for the same things those films were going for, but what this film IS going for failed to interest me. I have no nostalgia for the 50s, I get easily annoyed at convoluted plots with a bunch of names I don't remember, and I just didn't like how clean and safe this felt on a technical level. This is a vanilla film, which is fine, but it's also a vanilla story other than some occasional cool thematic concepts and fun name reveals (a game of telephone between two characters is especially great). 

If you want a safe version of a police procedural mystery type thing, this is going to be just for you. If you haven't seen any films like this, this is actually not a bad place to start! This just really isn't for me. 

Blu-ray

Name: The Devil Wears Prada
Review:
Pretty disappointed with this one. I was hoping for at least an entertaining ride, but this felt repetitive and out of ideas quickly. The attempts at characters are lame. Blunt especially was annoying, she feels like a cartoon character in how absurd her character traits are taken. Hathaway just plays herself. I do kind of like what Streep was doing, but it was not a performance worth watching the movie for. 

There are also many comments about the characters weight and size that were played up for laughs. I wasn't originally offended because of how I figured by the end it would show that all of that is absurd, but there is zero commentary about how toxic and sad that lifestyle is. In fact, this film glorifies a lot that it shouldn't. 

Now, I think some of the shots were cool, and I think the last 20 minutes are easily the most fun, electric parts of the film, but absolutely not worth the hour plus that comes before it. Don't waste your time with this one.

Hulu

Name: The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou
Review:
This is a strange Wes Anderson film to categorize. It feels like he's trying really hard to recapture the magic of The Royal Tenenbaums, which I haven't felt watching anything else by him. This makes this perhaps his most relentlessly quirky. The costume design, set design, and soundtrack are all taken to the Wes-ness extreme. This is clearly the focus of the film, with the story and characters taking a backseat. 

Considering how absorbed I got into the world, this is totally fine with me. I love the cast's absurd performances, the cute costumes, and the hilariously stilted plot. This movie has some of my favorite sequences in any of his films ever: the storming of the island, the pirates, and the story's set up. Unfortunately, this also has some of my least favorites sections. Bad, offensive jokes, boring digressions, and silly character decisions. 

Still, I had a great time by the end. I especially love the last third. I wish this were a little shorter, tighter, and emotional, but I'd still recommend this to any Wes Anderson fan. 

APV

Name: She's Gotta Have It
Review:
What an underrated film! 

This is a funny, progressive, and shocking commentary on female sexuality. Somehow, this is still progressive 36 years later. This covers a lot of ground, from the impossibility for women to be "appropriately" sexual, the various ways men hide their perversity, as well as a meditation on the "point" of sex in the first place. This film smoothly integrates these themes (and more) into a slick, stylish, and hilarious final product. 

This film is masterful at balancing the social commentary with a light-hearted tone. This alone is impressive, but the inclusion of outstanding, unforgettable cinematography pushes this further up my list of favorite directorial debuts ever. 

Unfortunately, there were a few things that didn't work for me here, namely the acting. Spike Lee is hilarious and perfect here, but everyone else felt stilted and awkward. I especially think the form of having the characters talk to the camera amplified the film's occasional amateurity. I also think the pacing, especially in the first half, was a little off ( I was extremely tired when I saw this though so take this with a grain of salt ). 

Overall, I love this. This is so smart and full of style while managing to be an amazingly fun time. I HIGHLY recommend this, despite the mediocre ratings. 

ALSO WOOHOO 500TH FILM EVER

Netflix

Name: Perfect Blue
Review:
On my first watch of this film, I made the mistake of reading a long letterboxd review and letting that influence my score. After a rewatch, I think I slightly overrated this. 

What makes this film so unbelievable is its pacing, editing, and ability to suck you into a dissociative nightmare. I've never seen something so adept at putting you in the head of someone going crazy. The way scenes flow into each other, even before shit hits the fan, is mesmerizing and terrifying.
