This is one of the most effective psychological horror movies I've seen from the PSYCHOLOGICAL perspective. I questioned my sanity, even on a rewatch.

I also love this film's thematic explorations, but I think I overrated their organization and clarity. This is sort of a mess, which lends to outstanding psychological horror, but it sacrifices just a touch of cohesion. This doesn't go deep into anything, instead painting a broad portrait of hell for a young woman in the limelight. I think this film would have benefitted from a more thoughtful, intentional descent into madness, like "Synecdoche, New York", "The Lighthouse", or even "First Reformed". 

Still, this is such a marvel. The music is so eerie, with one of the tracks sounding like someone breathing. I also love how muted and dead the color palette gets as the film goes on, though I think visually this could have benefitted from being slightly trippier. This is one of the best horror movies I've ever seen, and I think it's one of the more accessible horror films I've seen as well. 

If you want your mind fried, this is an outstanding choice. 

Blu ray

Name: Princess Mononoke
Review:
I have fond memories of watching this when I was a preteen and thinking this is the coolest movie ever. The first scene of gore is engrained in my brain for how badass I thought it was at the time, and it was fun to relive that.

This film is GREAT, but it's something I respect more than love. This is some of the best animation in Miyazaki's filmography, the deepest world building, and greatest thematic complexity. I love how nuanced each party's motivations are, and how this film has characters with black, white, and gray morality. 

Unfortunately, this just isn't my kind of thing. I wasn't always completely engaged. I loved more of the world building stuff than the saving-the-world plot aspects. I also think the main character is kind of uninteresting, though I do understand his thematic purpose for the story. 

This is a nearly flawless film that I didn't quite connect with personally. I still recommend this to anyone who wants a darker, angsty Miyazaki film. This might be your favorite (Totoro is still the best imo).

HBO Max

Name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
Review:
Every time I see this film I like the first half less and the second half more. Somehow I cried TWICE in this movie on this viewing. This is one of the best blends of arthouse and mainstream of all time, if not the best ever. This is also one of the slickest, sneakiest, most complete philosophical odysseys I've ever seen. There are so many reasons to love this film, even on a fourth watch. 

GO SEE IT IF YOU HAVEN'T 

AMC Creve Coeur

Name: The Sixth Sense
Review:
There's a lot to love about this film, but I'm surprised to say that Unbreakable is better. 

I was surprised to see that this film often lacked the Shayamalisms that I love so much in Unbreakable. The cinematography was sometimes, unfortunately, bland. Especially in the first third. 

I also think that the tone is not quite the Shayamalan I want to see. This is too Hollywood for me. I got the vibe everything was going to work out in the end, which I don't think complemented the story well. 

Still, I really really enjoyed this. This had less awkward, strange dialogue, the tension built up gradually and nicely, and the choices for what we do and do not see are purposeful and add to the brilliant final twist. Osment provides one of the greatest child performances of all time, right next to the kid from The Shining. 

Overall, this is a fun thriller I would call a must see, but unfortunately this doesn't provide quite a unique enough experience for me to give a higher rating. 

APV

Name: Fallen Angels
Review:
What a beautiful mess. 

Maybe the vibiest movie I've ever seen? The aesthetic of this is something else. I've never seen anything like this, it captures the surreality of the middle of the night masterfully. From a subconscious level this is basically perfect. 

Where this film gets me is what it's saying and doing with its style. I think it's attempting to explore the strange beauty of fleeting connection with other people, but I don't think the appeal of this film is easily analyzed. This feels like an anti-Haneke where the strength of the film's mood triggers unexplainable feelings (as opposed to the strength being the calculatedness of expressing a concrete philosophical theme). 

This might grow on me as I think (feel?) about it more, but the pure vibes propelling the film is not something I've experienced much before (something like Apocalypse Now has the vibeyness all over the place, but the trippy-vibeyness supplements a greater idea rather than being the only thing there). 

I don't know what of this is normal Wong Kar-Wai stuff and how much is just this film, but either way I'm super excited to check out the rest of his stuff. It's possible I rewatch this and it's a 9. I DON'T KNOW HOW TO FEEL.

HBO Max

Name: Burn After Reading
Review:
This is SUCH an amazing surprise for me. I've tried two of the Coen's highest rated films and neither of them left a major impression on me. Somehow, THIS film has me excited to see the rest of their work. 

This is one of the funniest movies I've ever seen. The characters are so detailed and well-defined to a hilarious extent. Of course Pitt steals the show, but everyone has their moments. This isn't always a laugh-out-loud funny kind of movie, but the growing absurdity of the story and how straight the film plays it just kills me omg. It's funny to me in the same way After Hours is, if that makes sense. 

This manages to also be visually masterful. There are some amazing shots here, probably the best shot comedy I've ever seen. 

I also love how beautifully this balances all the perspectives. The narrative framing which becomes more prevalent at the end of the film (don't want to spoil it, but it involves J.K. Simmons) is perfect. 

Thematically I see its similarities to Fargo and No Country, but for some reason it works so much better for me here. What's the point? What is there to learn? I don't really know! 

HULU

Name: Days of Heaven
Review:
Malick just might make my favorite movie ever, I can feel it. 

This is the kind of 4/5 film that's disappointing. This had the potential to be a MASTERPIECE, but unfortunately a scant story massively holds this back. There is, like, 70 minutes of story in this 95 minutes of movie. This would have dragged unbelievably bad if everything separate from the script weren't almost literally perfect. 

Technically, this is literally one of the best films I've ever seen. Almost every shot is perfect. This manages to visually represent both Heaven and Hell perfectly. It's not something that can be described, just watch this movie and tell me this isn't one of the best visual spectacles of all time. 

This is one of the best directed films I have ever seen, but a good story and complicated characters are a must to make a masterpiece. While visually not quite as good, I still prefer Badlands because of the better balance between visuals, themes, and and an interesting story. 

Days of Heaven is style over substance, but the style is so mind-blowing that the slowness didn't bother me too much. With a beefier story this would be one of the greatest ever, but as it stands, it's still worth it for EVERYONE to watch. 

If you wouldn't get bored in an Art Museum, you're going to love this. 

APV

Name: The Twilight Saga: Eclipse
Review:
After marathoning the most of this series about two years ago ( SKIPPING THIS MOVIE ) I came out with the impression that this series is slightly over-hated. Sure, Edward is a little pedophilic and the acting is less than amazing, but the visuals are often stylized and the atmosphere is unique. We finally decided to fill my Twilight hole and watch the final movie I haven't seen.

Oh my god. This is terrible. 

This is the worst romance I have ever seen. How the hell is Team Jacob a thing? How am I SO STRONGLY Team Edward when EDWARD IS OVER 100 YEARS OLD ATTEMPTING TO COURT A 18 YEAR OLD??? I am on team 100 year old. BELLA, CHOOSE THE 100 YEAR OLD PLEASE.

Jacob is rapey and gross. The acting is the worst of any of the films. Bella's dad, the best part of these films, is underused. The film feels like complete filler. It was boring and way too long. This gets a half star for the color palette, a couple cool shots, and Bella's dad. Write a spin-off series about the dad please. Thank you. 

Blu-ray

Name: 28 Days Later
Review:
What a great time!! This movie makes kind of no sense, the characters act inconsistently, and this feels just a hair too long, but other than that this is a total blast. Maybe my favorite zombie movie. 

I love the lo-fi presentation, it totally suits this being the end of the world. It feels purposeful rather than due to budgetary restrictions. I also love the direction the story goes, creating commentary on trust, the purpose of living, and toxic masculinity.

There are some AMAZING ideas here, like how dreams are portrayed, as well as the initial montage of the main character leaving the hospital (even though, again, this made no sense). I also actually like the ending, because to my interpretation its ambiguous. 

This features so many charismatic performances, brilliant visual style, and an interesting and unpredictable story. If you're looking for a fun action/zombie/stylized film, this is a great choice if you can look past the plot holes and terrible character choices. 

HBO Max

Name: Coherence
Review:
I want this exact premise and basic ideas done again. This concept has the potential to reach the heights of mindbenders like "Primer", but a lack of polish holds this back. 

One of the coolest things about this film is one of the worst things. All of the acting and dialogue are improvised, with only the filmmakers knowing all the twists and turns of the story. This is a thrilling idea, but requires a lot of improvisational skill. Sometimes, it works amazingly. I was going to compliment this script for how realistic some of the dialogue sounds, but I know now that that realism is because these are literally people having a dinner party. It isn't acting. Unfortunately, when things get weird and people do some strange things (as per their "character card" directions given to them by the director), some scenes feel like acting in porn. 

"No!!! Don't go in there!!!" 
"Why?"
"Because!! Just don't!!!!"

 It can be distractingly bad. Even when it isn't like porn, it's sometimes the vibe of what I would imagine a murder mystery dinner party thing would be like: more fun to be a part of than to watch. 

This film has amazing ideas. It tackles some interesting philosophical dilemmas and has some interesting things to say about the fragility of friendship, but the execution can make the film feel like a series of bullet points rather than something smooth and cohesive. 

I think the choices for visual style (cramped, close shots with many cuts) is smart, but some of the lighting is also distractingly bad. Some faces look like ghosts. It is also a tad frustrating how difficult it is to hear the characters talking, especially in the beginning.

I dunno, this had A LOT of potential, but I think this cast and crew could not quite pull off the ambition of the project. I would love to see a do-over made by a more experienced/talented group of people, as well as a higher tolerance toward reshooting. This is still a fun, mind-blowing time I would recommend if you're looking to scratch that "Primer" or "Upstream Color" itch.

Name: The Thing
Review:
This is pretty cool! This isn't my favorite horror movie or anything, but the development of atmosphere, paranoia, and hopelessness is well done for sure. These characters are memorable, there's some INSANE body horror oh my god, and the plot has surprisingly few holes in it for a concept as outrageous as this. 

It could have just been that I watched the cropped, quiet rerelease in theaters, but the vibes didn't quite hit as hard as I was hoping for them to. The positives of this for me boil down to this films ability to balance the insane supernatural stuff with the grounded pragmatism of these researchers. Also, Childs and MacReady are the best. So memorable. 

Marcus Chesterfield

Name: Turning Red
Review:
I get the hype, it's just not my thing. 

My favorite parts of this are when the film goes outside its Pixar box. I love the anime inspired animation, the occasionally risqué (for Pixar) subject matter, and how nonchalantly this film handles diversity.

Unfortunately, the humor only rarely appeals to me, the characters are not memorable, and the film takes a sanitary stance on parents having unreasonably high standards for their children. I also felt disappointed at how loose the metaphor for puberty the panda becomes by the end of the film. 

I would love to see this director do something outside of the Pixar limitations. As it stands, this is a serviceable kids movie if you're willing to turn your brain off. I wish this went further with its concept. 

Disney Plus

Name: Sweet Smell of Success
Review:
I think this is technically my first Noir! I liked it!!!

This dialogue is some of my favorite from an older film. It's razor sharp and wickedly mean. I have a pretty bad memory, but I can still remember some quips word for word. 

I also love the performances from the two leads. What a slimy pair. I love how terrible they are to each other, it's super fun to watch.

I did have some issues, like the confusing first 25 minutes, the sanitary ending, and less than stellar pacing. I also think that these characters are not too complicated, which makes me feel like the film is a little underdone. These characters are a dark, DARK shade of gray, a choice which makes the film slightly less interesting to me. 

Overall, I enjoyed this a ton! I'm hyped to explore more films from this era. 

APV
